FILM/DIRECTORS/Agnès-Varda.title: Agnès Varda
FILM/DIRECTORS/Agnès-Varda.content:
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Left Bank
Agnès Varda came to cinema from photography and art history, not film criticism, and it shows. Her work has the texture of someone who learned to see before she learned to edit—attentive to surfaces, bodies, spaces, the way light falls on a face. She’s grouped with the New Wave but belongs more precisely to the Left Bank: the literary, documentary-inflected wing that included Alain Resnais and Chris Marker. Where the Cahiers directors dismantled Hollywood genre, Varda blended fiction and documentary to ask different questions—about women, labor, memory, and who gets to be seen.
Modernist problem
How can cinema represent women’s lived experience without reducing them to spectacle, narrative function, or symbol?
Position within the French New Wave
Varda precedes and parallels the New Wave rather than emerging from it. La Pointe Courte (1955) predates Breathless by five years. She never wrote for Cahiers; her formation was visual and anthropological, not cinephilic. While Godard and Truffaut reworked genre through quotation and rupture, Varda developed a hybrid method—documentary observation, staged fiction, essayistic voiceover—that pointed cinema toward embodiment and social structure rather than film history.
Feminist modernism
Varda was making feminist cinema before feminist film theory had a vocabulary for it. In (1962) Cléo from 5 to 7, she tracks a woman’s movement from object to subject, using real-time duration and shifting perspective to challenge the male gaze. In (1965) Le Bonheur, she exposes how patriarchal ideology operates through beauty itself—saturated color and pastoral imagery masking the disposability of women. In (1985) Vagabond, she takes the method to its extreme: a woman who refuses to become an image at all, filmed through the testimonies of those who failed to see her.
What makes her feminism modernist is the method: she doesn’t argue, she shows. Gendered power becomes visible through form—through what the camera lingers on, what it skips, whose interiority it grants access to.
Form and method
Varda moves fluidly between documentary and fiction, pioneering what later gets called the essay film. Her toolkit:

Observational realism and staged tableaux
Voiceover reflection and direct address
Collage montage and archival material
Color as ideological critique

The formal experimentation stays grounded in empathy. She’s interested in people, not techniques.
Memory, place, and everyday life
Across her career, Varda treats memory as embodied and spatial. Daguerreotypes films her own neighborhood; (1985) Vagabond tracks a drifter through the French countryside; (2000) The Gleaners and I finds poetry in scavenging and aging, while turning the camera on Varda’s own hands, hair, and body — making the filmmaker herself subject to the same attention she gives everyone else. Identity emerges from labor, geography, and economic precarity—not as abstraction but as lived accumulation.
Political documentary and the outside observer
During the years Varda lived in Los Angeles with Demy (1968–1970), she made Black Panthers (1968), a 28-minute documentary shot at rallies in Oakland demanding Huey Newton’s release. The film shows Varda’s documentary method applied to a political movement she’s observing from outside — as a French woman, a white woman, a filmmaker with no claim on the struggle. She lets the speakers (Kathleen Cleaver, Bobby Seale, Stokely Carmichael) occupy the frame on their own terms, giving their rhetoric room to work without editorial framing. The film captures the Panthers at their peak of public visibility, before the FBI’s COINTELPRO campaign dismantled the organization — a historical context that gives the images an elegiac weight Varda couldn’t have anticipated. It’s minor Varda, but it fills out the picture: the same attentive, empathetic observation she brings to Parisian shop owners and French gleaners, directed at an American revolutionary movement.
Legacy
Varda pioneered feminist cinema, reshaped documentary practice, and expanded the possibilities of autobiographical filmmaking. Her influence runs through Chantal Akerman, Claire Denis, Céline Sciamma, Greta Gerwig, and Kirsten Johnson—filmmakers who make women’s experience visible without reducing it to issue or symbol.
Her cinema proves that modernism can be playful, ethical, politically alert, and deeply humane.

FILM/DIRECTORS/Akira-Kurosawa.title: Akira Kurosawa
FILM/DIRECTORS/Akira-Kurosawa.content:
Movement: Japanese Golden Age Cinema
Akira Kurosawa made cinema that moves. His frames are dense with weather, bodies, and moral urgency; his editing is precise and propulsive; his stories are structured around individuals forced into situations that demand action and reveal character. He absorbed Hollywood’s storytelling energy — he admired John Ford, he loved Westerns — and combined it with a formal ambition, a visual scale, and a humanist seriousness that exceeded anything Hollywood was producing. Kurosawa is often called the most “Western” of Japanese directors, which is both true (his narrative structures are legible to global audiences) and misleading (his themes — duty, shame, the relationship between master and student, the individual within the group — are deeply Japanese). He is the supreme practitioner of the movement-image in Japanese cinema: his films believe that action reveals character, that moral choice is possible, and that cinema’s power lies in kinetic force.
Modernist problem
How can cinema dramatize the collision between individual conscience and social structure — without simplifying either?
Kurosawa’s answer: genre as moral laboratory. The samurai film, the crime thriller, the historical epic — these aren’t containers for adventure but testing grounds for ethical philosophy. (1954) Seven Samurai asks what happens when skill meets altruism meets futility. (1950) Rashomon asks whether truth survives the telling. (1963) High and Low asks what a good man owes a society that would destroy him. The genre framework provides the action; Kurosawa uses the action to pose questions that have no clean answers.
Editing and movement
Kurosawa’s editing is among the most dynamic in cinema. He pioneered the use of multiple cameras — shooting the same scene from several angles simultaneously — which gave him an editing grammar of unprecedented flexibility. His action sequences (the rain-soaked battle in Seven Samurai, the chase through Yokohama in (1949) Stray Dog) are built through rhythmic cutting that accelerates and decelerates with the physical energy of the scene. But his quiet scenes are equally precise: the slow accumulation of tension in High and Low’s first half (a single room, a moral dilemma, the sweat on Gondo’s face) demonstrates that Kurosawa’s kinetic genius extends to stillness under pressure.
He also understood weather as a formal element — rain, wind, dust, heat — in ways that go beyond atmosphere. The rain in Seven Samurai’s final battle doesn’t set a mood; it’s a compositional force that dissolves the boundary between bodies and landscape, turning the battle into a churning abstraction of mud and motion.
Widescreen composition
Kurosawa’s shift to widescreen in the late 1950s unlocked his most sophisticated compositional work. The wider frame allowed him to stage scenes in deep space — characters at different depths, architectural elements creating natural frames-within-frames — while maintaining the kinetic editing that defined his style. High and Low uses the wide frame to map social hierarchy spatially: Gondo’s hilltop house looking down on the city, the kidnapper looking up. The composition is the argument.
Humanism and moral gravity
Kurosawa’s films carry moral weight without moralizing. His heroes are flawed, his villains comprehensible, his resolutions rarely clean. Rashomon’s famous structure (four contradictory accounts of the same event) doesn’t resolve into a single truth; it asks whether objective truth is accessible at all — and then, in the final scene, offers an act of kindness that doesn’t answer the epistemological question but suggests that compassion might matter more than certainty. This connects to Epistemological doubt but with a different emphasis: where European modernists let doubt stand as the endpoint, Kurosawa pushes through doubt toward provisional, fragile moral action.

FILM/DIRECTORS/Alain-Resnais.title: Alain Resnais
FILM/DIRECTORS/Alain-Resnais.content:
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Left Bank
Alain Resnais came to features from documentary—most famously Night and Fog (1955), a confrontation with the Holocaust that remains one of cinema’s most devastating uses of archival footage. That background shapes everything: his films treat memory as material to be structured, trauma as something that intrudes rather than explains, time as layered rather than sequential. He’s the New Wave’s architect, building films like you’d build a fugue.
Modernist problem
How can cinema represent memory, trauma, and consciousness when time is fractured, perception is unstable, and experience resists narration?
Position within the French New Wave
Resnais emerged from documentary practice rather than cinephile criticism. He never wrote for Cahiers; his collaborators were novelists—Marguerite Duras, Alain Robbe-Grillet—whose literary experiments shaped his approach to structure and language. Where Godard dismantles genre and Truffaut reworks autobiography, Resnais constructs formal systems: films organized by recurrence, counterpoint, and temporal ambiguity rather than plot.
Memory as structure
In (1959) Hiroshima mon amour and (1961) Last Year at Marienbad, memory isn’t flashback or backstory—it’s the organizing principle. Past and present interpenetrate; fantasy and recollection blur. Montage creates association rather than sequence. Characters exist as constellations of fragments, their identities provisional, shaped by what can’t be fully recalled or resolved (see Memory as structure in modernist cinema).
Hiroshima intercuts documentary atrocity with intimate encounter, testing whether private grief can approach historical catastrophe. Marienbad goes further, constructing a narrative where the central event may or may not have happened, where repetition multiplies possibility rather than confirming fact.
Trauma and history
Night and Fog established Resnais’s lifelong concern with how cinema represents what exceeds representation. That inquiry continues in fiction: Muriel (1963) addresses the Algerian War through fractured editing and domestic claustrophobia; Je t’aime, je t’aime (1968) uses science fiction to stage memory as recursive paralysis.
History in Resnais isn’t context—it’s an intrusive force that destabilizes private life and narrative form alike.
Form
Resnais treats cinema as a phenomenological instrument. Sound and image operate in counterpoint; narration contradicts what we see; repetition generates uncertainty rather than clarity. His tracking shots map spaces the way thoughts map memory—circling, returning, never quite arriving. The comparison to music is apt: his structures are closer to composition than storytelling.
Later work
Resnais’s late career introduced playfulness—Smoking/No Smoking (1993), On connaît la chanson (1997)—but the underlying concerns remain. Chance, contingency, branching narratives. Even when the tone lightens, time stays layered, memory stays unreliable.
Legacy
Resnais helped define art cinema’s departure from classical narrative. His influence runs through Krzysztof Kieślowski, Wong Kar-wai, Charlie Kaufman, and Apichatpong Weerasethakul—filmmakers who treat cinema as a medium for modeling consciousness rather than recording events.

FILM/DIRECTORS/Alfred-Hitchcock.title: Alfred Hitchcock
FILM/DIRECTORS/Alfred-Hitchcock.content:
Movement: Classical Hollywood cinema
Alfred Hitchcock worked inside the classical system more completely than any director in this vault so far and did more to undermine it from within. He accepted every convention — genre contracts, narrative causality, star performances, invisible editing — and used them to produce experiences that classical cinema’s ideology of transparency was designed to prevent: implication, discomfort, the suspicion that looking itself is dangerous. His films don’t break the rules. They use the rules to reveal what the rules are hiding.
Formal problem
How can cinema make the spectator complicit — not just engaged or entertained but guilty — while operating entirely within commercial entertainment?
Hitchcock’s answer: control the gaze. Classical editing tells the audience where to look; Hitchcock weaponizes that authority. He shows you what a character sees, then shows you seeing it, then makes you aware that your pleasure in looking is the same pleasure that drives the voyeur, the stalker, the killer. Suspense isn’t a technique — it’s a moral condition. You know the bomb is under the table. You’re watching anyway.
The gaze
Hitchcock’s central formal innovation is the systematic manipulation of point-of-view to implicate the spectator. The shot/reverse-shot structure — the basic grammar of classical cinema — becomes, in his hands, a trap. When Scottie watches Madeleine in Vertigo, the film positions you inside his obsession. When Norman watches Marion through the peephole in Psycho, you’re watching through the same hole. When Jeff watches his neighbors in Rear Window, his apartment window is a movie screen and you’re sitting in his chair.
This isn’t self-reflexivity in the modernist sense — Hitchcock never breaks the illusion. He uses the illusion’s own machinery to reveal what it’s doing to you. The apparatus remains invisible; the guilt it produces becomes visible.
Suspense vs. surprise
Hitchcock’s famous distinction: surprise is a bomb exploding unexpectedly; suspense is knowing the bomb is there and watching characters talk over it for five minutes. Suspense requires information — the audience must know more than the characters. This reverses classical cinema’s default, where the audience discovers information alongside the protagonist. By giving the audience superior knowledge, Hitchcock transforms them from passive followers into anxious accomplices. You want to warn the character. You can’t. You keep watching. The pleasure of suspense is inseparable from the helplessness it produces.
Architecture and space
Hitchcock’s spaces are never neutral. The Bates house stacked above the motel in Psycho (mother above, son below). The apartment as surveillance station in Rear Window. The vertiginous bell tower in Vertigo. The confined dinner party in Rope. Manderley’s corridors in Rebecca. These are architectures of psychology — spaces that produce the emotional conditions the films explore. Hitchcock builds his sets the way he builds his plots: every element doing double duty, every space expressing the power relations it contains.
The wrong man and the double
A recurring structure: an innocent person pulled into a guilty world, or two people linked by a symmetry they can’t escape. Strangers on a Train makes the doubling explicit — two men, two murders, a compact. Shadow of a Doubt mirrors uncle and niece. Vertigo builds an entire false identity. The wrong-man scenario isn’t a plot device; it’s Hitchcock’s way of saying that guilt isn’t earned — it’s structural. You don’t have to do anything to become implicated. Proximity is enough.
The blonde
Hitchcock’s women — Grace Kelly, Kim Novak, Tippi Hedren, Eva Marie Saint, Ingrid Bergman, Joan Fontaine — are constructed images: cool, blonde, controlled surfaces that the films subject to disruption, surveillance, violence, and desire. The blonde isn’t a type; she’s a formal element — an image the male gaze assembles and the film then interrogates or destroys. This connects Hitchcock’s work to Feminist frameworks in cinema and to Lynch’s treatment of the female image as something simultaneously worshipped and shattered.
Relation to the vault
Hitchcock is the bridge between Classical Hollywood cinema and the modernist directors who follow. His manipulation of the gaze prefigures everything from Antonioni’s architecture of alienation to Lynch’s voyeuristic closets. His treatment of identity — Scottie remaking Judy, Norman becoming Mother, Uncle Charlie’s double life — predicts the identity fractures that modernist and postmodern cinema will take as their central subject. His formal perfectionism operates within the classical system, but the experiences it produces — guilt, implication, the unease of looking — belong to modernism.

FILM/DIRECTORS/Andrei-Tarkovsky.title: Andrei Tarkovsky
FILM/DIRECTORS/Andrei-Tarkovsky.content:
Movement: Soviet Poetic Cinema
Andrei Tarkovsky made seven features in twenty-five years, and each one feels like it took a lifetime to create. Working within and against Soviet cinema, he developed a filmmaking practice devoted to time, memory, and spiritual longing—cinema as a way of sculpting duration itself. His films move slowly, often wordlessly, through landscapes saturated with water, fire, and decay. They resist interpretation while demanding contemplation. Tarkovsky believed cinema’s unique power was its ability to capture time in its passage, and his work makes that belief visible: watching his films, you feel time differently than you do elsewhere.
Modernist problem
How can cinema make visible the invisible—memory, spiritual longing, the soul’s movements—without reducing them to symbol or narrative?
Tarkovsky’s answer: duration. By holding shots long past their narrative function, by letting the camera drift through spaces, by saturating the image with elemental textures (water, fire, earth, wind), he creates a cinema where time itself becomes the medium of meaning.
Sculpting in time
Tarkovsky’s phrase for what cinema does: sculpting in time. The filmmaker works with time the way a sculptor works with marble—shaping it, revealing what’s already there. This isn’t metaphor for Tarkovsky; it’s ontology. Cinema’s essence is its capacity to preserve and shape duration. Every other art form represents time; only cinema captures it.
This belief produces his distinctive formal approach: long takes that let events unfold in real duration, tracking shots that move through space with the slowness of memory or dream, a refusal to cut when cutting would provide the usual rhythmic satisfactions.
The elements
Water is everywhere in Tarkovsky: rain, rivers, puddles, flooded rooms, dripping ceilings. Fire burns. Wind moves through grass. These aren’t symbols to be decoded; they’re presences to be experienced. Tarkovsky loads his images with elemental texture until the material world seems to exceed its own materiality—matter infused with spirit, or at least with the longing for spirit.
The decay matters too: peeling walls, rusting metal, abandoned buildings. Tarkovsky films a world that is passing away, and the passing is part of the beauty.
Memory and dream
Tarkovsky’s films move between present, past, and dream without clear markers. Mirror interweaves three generations and multiple temporal layers; Stalker occurs in a Zone where the laws of space and time don’t apply. Memory in these films isn’t flashback—it’s the continuous presence of the past, the way childhood and loss inhabit the present moment.
This connects him to Alain Resnais and the Left Bank, though Tarkovsky’s approach is less literary, more elemental. Where Resnais constructs elaborate temporal architectures, Tarkovsky lets time flow like water.
Faith and doubt
Tarkovsky was a believer—Russian Orthodox, serious about it—but his films don’t preach. They long. The spiritual dimension is present as absence, as desire, as the pressure of transcendence against a world that can’t quite receive it. Stalker’s Zone promises to grant your deepest wish, but no one dares enter the Room. Andrei Rublev follows an icon painter through violence and despair to an act of creation that may or may not redeem.
This is spiritual cinema for skeptics: faith as problem rather than answer.
Against Soviet cinema
Tarkovsky worked within the Soviet system but against its aesthetic norms. Socialist Realism demanded clarity, optimism, collective purpose. Tarkovsky offered ambiguity, melancholy, individual spiritual crisis. The authorities tolerated him because his international prestige was useful; they also made his life difficult, delayed his projects, and eventually drove him into exile.
His films aren’t political in any direct sense, but their existence was a political act: proof that art could pursue its own necessities within a system designed to prevent exactly that.
Legacy
Tarkovsky’s influence runs through art cinema globally: Béla Tarr, Terrence Malick, Theo Angelopoulos, Lars von Trier (who both worships and parodies him). He proved that cinema could be contemplative without being inert, spiritual without being naive, slow without being empty. His films remain among the most demanding in the canon—and among the most rewarding for those willing to submit to their rhythms.

FILM/DIRECTORS/Billy-Wilder.title: Billy Wilder
FILM/DIRECTORS/Billy-Wilder.content:
Movement: Classical Hollywood cinema
Billy Wilder worked inside the classical system as completely as Hitchcock — studio productions, star vehicles, genre contracts — and used it to say things the system was designed to prevent. Where Hitchcock subverts from the camera (manipulating the gaze, making the spectator complicit), Wilder subverts from the screenplay (manipulating dialogue, making characters say one thing while meaning another). His weapon is language: what people say, what they don’t say, what they say while lying, and the gap between wit and despair that his best films inhabit simultaneously.
Wilder came from Vienna via Berlin, fled the Nazis, learned English as a second language, and became one of Hollywood’s greatest writers of English dialogue. The émigré perspective matters: he saw American culture from outside, loved its energy and surfaces, and understood its capacity for self-deception better than most native-born filmmakers. His cynicism isn’t nihilism — it’s the clarity of someone who has seen worse and knows that charm, comfort, and institutional smoothness can conceal almost anything.
Formal problem
How can cinema tell the truth about corruption, exploitation, and self-deception while remaining entertaining — while operating inside the very system it’s diagnosing?
Wilder’s answer: the screenplay. His scripts are constructed with a precision that approaches architecture — every line serves multiple purposes, every scene turns on what characters conceal. The wit isn’t decoration; it’s the mechanism by which uncomfortable truths become speakable. Characters in Wilder films are often brilliant talkers, and the brilliance of their talk is frequently what keeps them (and the audience) from seeing what’s actually happening.
The screenwriter as auteur
Wilder co-wrote every film he directed. This makes him unusual in auteur theory, which privileges visual style and directorial control. Wilder’s authorship is textual — it lives in structure, dialogue rhythm, and the ironic gap between what’s said and what’s meant. His visual style is efficient, classical, and largely invisible. The camera doesn’t call attention to itself because the writing is doing the work. This doesn’t make him less of a formalist than Hitchcock; it makes him a formalist of a different order — one whose medium is language rather than the gaze.
Cynicism and sentiment
Wilder’s reputation as a cynic is half right. His films are relentlessly clear-eyed about human selfishness, institutional corruption, and the American talent for packaging exploitation as opportunity. But the cynicism coexists with real tenderness — Baxter’s decency in The Apartment, Sugar’s vulnerability in Some Like It Hot, even Norma Desmond’s grandeur in delusion. The films don’t resolve the tension between cynicism and sentiment; they hold both, and the holding is what makes them feel true.
Hollywood and self-knowledge
Wilder made two of cinema’s greatest films about its own industry. (1950) Sunset Boulevard anatomizes Hollywood’s relationship to its own past — the discarded star, the forgotten mansion, the young writer who sells himself for comfort. It’s a self-reflexive film made entirely within classical conventions, which is what makes it so devastating: the system is diagnosing itself in its own language. The Apartment performs a similar operation on corporate America: the machine that organizes private life for institutional convenience, and the one person inside it who still has a conscience.
Relation to the vault
Wilder occupies the position of the other great classical saboteur alongside Hitchcock. Both work within Classical Hollywood cinema to reveal what the system conceals. But their methods are complementary: Hitchcock controls the image, Wilder controls the word. Hitchcock makes you see; Wilder makes you listen. Together they demonstrate that classical Hollywood, at its best, could produce work as formally rigorous and thematically disturbing as anything in modernist cinema — without ever announcing itself as art.

FILM/DIRECTORS/Chantal-Akerman.title: Chantal Akerman
FILM/DIRECTORS/Chantal-Akerman.content:
Movement: Modernist cinema
Chantal Akerman saw Godard’s Pierrot le fou at fifteen in Brussels and decided to make films. What she made bore almost no resemblance to Godard. Where the New Wave directors quoted, cut, accelerated, and played, Akerman held. She held the camera on doorways, on corridors, on women’s hands performing domestic labor. She held until the activity that classical cinema skips — cooking, cleaning, dressing, waiting — became the primary material of the film. She held until duration itself became the argument.
Akerman doesn’t belong to any single movement in this vault. She inherits from the Left Bank’s hybrid of documentary and fiction, from structural film’s interest in duration and repetition, from feminist cinema’s insistence that women’s experience constitutes valid cinematic material. But she synthesizes these into something her own: a cinema of quotidian attention so sustained and so precise that the everyday becomes monumental — and the monumental, when it arrives, registers as a rupture in a system rather than a dramatic event.
Modernist problem
How can cinema make women’s domestic labor — the invisible, repetitive, structuring work that organizes daily life — visible as both subject and form?
Akerman’s answer: duration. Film the labor in real time. Don’t compress it, don’t aestheticize it, don’t explain it. Let the spectator sit inside the time that domestic work actually takes. The boredom, the precision, the repetition — these aren’t obstacles to cinema; they’re the content. When the routine finally breaks, the break means something because you’ve lived inside the routine.
Duration as method
Akerman’s long takes aren’t the same as Antonioni’s or Tarkovsky’s, though all three directors use sustained duration. Antonioni holds to produce alienation — the long take empties space of human meaning. Tarkovsky holds to produce contemplation — the long take fills time with spiritual weight. Akerman holds to produce recognition: the gradual awareness that what you’re watching is labor, that the labor takes this long, that someone does it every day, and that cinema has always edited it out.
This connects to Transcendental style and slow cinema — Schrader places Jeanne Dielman on the “surveillance camera” axis, cinema approaching the condition of objective recording. But Akerman’s camera isn’t neutral. Its fixed position, its refusal to follow the character, its exact framing of doorways and kitchen counters — these are formal choices that produce a specific kind of attention. The camera doesn’t observe dispassionately; it insists on what it’s showing.
Architecture and the domestic
Akerman’s spaces are almost exclusively interiors — apartments, corridors, kitchens, bedrooms. These aren’t the monumental architectures of Antonioni’s EUR or Hitchcock’s Manderley. They’re the spaces where women spend their time: functional, confined, organized by routine. Architecture in Akerman’s work doesn’t externalize alienation or produce Gothic dread. It externalizes structure — the spatial organization of a life governed by repetition, obligation, and the maintenance of order.
The apartment in Jeanne Dielman is the vault’s most complete example of domestic architecture as formal system. Every room has a function. Every surface is maintained. The camera knows where everything goes because Jeanne knows where everything goes. When objects start to shift — a lid left off, a button missed, a light left on — the architecture registers the disturbance before the character does.
Feminist formalism
Akerman is the filmmaker Varda’s legacy note points toward — the next generation of feminist cinema. But where Varda’s feminism operates through empathy, observation, and playful hybridity, Akerman’s operates through structure. She doesn’t advocate for women’s experience; she forces the spectator to inhabit the time of that experience. The political argument is in the duration, not in the content. You don’t need to be told that domestic labor is undervalued. You need to feel how long it takes to peel potatoes for twenty minutes.

FILM/DIRECTORS/Chris-Marker.title: Chris Marker
FILM/DIRECTORS/Chris-Marker.content:
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Left Bank
Chris Marker may be the most elusive major filmmaker of the twentieth century. He rarely gave interviews, used pseudonyms, and represented himself with a cartoon cat. What we have are the films: essay-documentaries that treat images as artifacts, memory as unstable, and cinema as a way of thinking rather than storytelling. He’s the architect of the essay film, and his influence runs through everything from video art to speculative documentary.
Modernist problem
How do images shape memory, history, and political consciousness—and what are the ethics of using them?
Position within the French New Wave
Marker belongs to the Left Bank alongside Alain Resnais and Agnès Varda, but he’s more radical than either. He abandons narrative almost entirely, developing an essayistic mode that blends documentary footage, fiction, philosophy, and political reflection. His films don’t tell stories; they think through images.
Memory and mediation
Marker’s central insight is that memory isn’t personal—it’s technological. Photographs, films, and archives don’t just record the past; they construct it. (1962) La Jetée, composed almost entirely of still photographs, makes this literal: a man travels through time via memory-images, and those images ultimately determine his fate. Sans Soleil (1983) extends the inquiry across continents, weaving travel footage, video games, Japanese culture, and revolutionary history into a meditation on what images do to remembrance.
In Marker, the archive is never neutral. It preserves and distorts, commemorates and entombs. See Memory as structure in modernist cinema and Photograph as death.
The essay film
Marker’s toolkit:

First-person or anonymous voiceover
Associative, digressive montage
Image-text disjunction (what we see doesn’t illustrate what we hear)
Temporal loops and recursions
Reflection over exposition

Meaning emerges through juxtaposition, not argument. The viewer has to assemble the pieces.
Politics without certainty
Marker was deeply political—anti-imperialist, engaged with revolutionary movements from Cuba to Chile to Japan. But his films resist propaganda. They foreground doubt, acknowledge the limits of representation, and treat political commitment as something that must be renewed rather than inherited. Le Joli Mai, Far from Vietnam, and the SLON collective films are activist works that remain skeptical of their own authority.
Technology and the future
Marker anticipated digital media theory by decades. His video works and installations (Immemory, Level Five) explore how new technologies reshape memory, identity, and historical consciousness. The archive in Marker is never stable—it’s contested, manipulable, ethically charged.
Tone
Despite the seriousness, Marker’s films are playful. Cats appear constantly—ironic mascots that deflate grand narratives. There’s wit in the voiceover, surprise in the editing. He takes history seriously without taking himself too seriously.
Legacy
Marker helped define the essay film as a major form. His influence extends through Harun Farocki, Patricio Guzmán, Werner Herzog, Hito Steyerl, and Arthur Jafa—filmmakers who use documentary as a mode of inquiry rather than reportage.
Links

Photograph as death
(1962) La Jetée

FILM/DIRECTORS/David-Lynch.title: David Lynch
FILM/DIRECTORS/David-Lynch.content:
Movement: American Postmodern Cinema
David Lynch makes films that operate like the unconscious. Not films about the unconscious — films that think the way dreams think: through substitution, displacement, condensation, and repetition. His characters inhabit genre frameworks (noir, horror, small-town melodrama) that feel simultaneously sincere and impossible, as if the conventions of American storytelling were being dreamed by someone who believes in them absolutely but can’t keep them stable. He emerged from the experimental art world, crossed into Hollywood, and carved out a space where the two became indistinguishable.
Formal problem
How can cinema represent psychic states — trauma, dissociation, guilt — that resist conscious articulation and rational narrative?
Lynch’s answer: dream logic as structural principle. Not dreams as content (Fellini uses dreams, Bergman uses dreams) but the logic of dreams — the way they substitute one person for another, collapse two spaces into one, loop back to their own beginnings — as the organizing principle of narrative itself.
Genre as psychic architecture
Lynch inhabits genre with absolute sincerity. His noirs are genuinely dark, his horrors genuinely frightening, his small-town idylls genuinely warm. He doesn’t quote genre (the Tarantino move) or pastiche it (the Coen Brothers move). He believes in it — and then subjects it to pressures that warp it from within. The white picket fence in Blue Velvet isn’t ironic; it’s both real and a lid over what’s underneath. Genre in Lynch is the shape consciousness takes when it can’t face what it knows.
Sound and darkness
Lynch is cinema’s great sound designer. Working with Angelo Badalamenti and Alan Splet, he builds sonic environments of sustained drones, industrial hum, and ambient threat that operate below conscious attention. The sound comes first; the image follows. His visual signature is darkness — not shadow (the expressionist tradition) but actual darkness, spaces so underlit that architecture dissolves and the screen becomes a field of black from which figures barely emerge. Sound and darkness together produce cinema as sensory immersion rather than visual narrative.
The double
Identity in Lynch splits. Fred becomes Pete (Lost Highway). Betty becomes Diane (Mulholland Drive). Laura Palmer is simultaneously victim and agent. The double isn’t a metaphor — it’s a structural principle. Consciousness, unable to integrate trauma, produces an alternate self, and the film occupies both selves without hierarchy. This connects to (1966) Persona’s merging faces but goes further: Bergman dissolves the boundary between two people; Lynch replaces one person with another entirely.
Americana
Lynch is the great poet of American surfaces — diners, highways, suburban lawns, curtains, cherry pie. He films these with genuine affection and with the knowledge that they conceal something unbearable. The surface isn’t false (the postmodern critique); it’s insufficient. Americana in Lynch is the dream that America dreams about itself, and his films track what happens when the dreamer starts to wake up.
Transcendental Meditation and the creative process
Lynch has practiced TM since 1973 — longer than he’s been a professional filmmaker. His creative philosophy, articulated in Catching the Big Fish, is inseparable from his meditative practice: ideas are fish; the deepest, most transformative ideas live in the deepest water; meditation expands the ocean. You don’t dive in search of specific solutions. You enliven the field of consciousness itself, sharpen intuition, and then go catch ideas.
This explains why Lynch’s films resist intellectual decoding. He doesn’t construct puzzles or embed hidden meanings. He retrieves material from below the surface of conscious thought and presents it with the conviction of a dream. The formal strategies — substitution, displacement, the double, the loop — are the natural shape of material from the deep. The Lady in the Radiator, the Mystery Man, BOB, the Red Room: these aren’t symbols to be decoded but catches to be experienced. “Lynch didn’t like his movies to be understood in intellectual terms, which in turn made his movies living examples of how to approach life: we aren’t supposed to understand this existence, but to simply accept it.”
The trajectory from Eraserhead to Inland Empire can be read as the progressive deepening of Lynch’s fishing: the container expands, the catches become stranger and less amenable to narrative packaging. The Straight Story represents a different kind of catch entirely — not from the turbulent depths but from the calm beneath them, what TM practitioners describe as the ocean of pure consciousness, pure peace.
Position between modernism and postmodernism
Lynch inherits Postmodernist cinema’s genre awareness, its collapse of high and low, its media reflexivity. But he retains Modernist cinema’s commitment to interiority — to the representation of consciousness, to the belief that cinema can access depths that resist articulation. His films aren’t ironic; they’re oneiric. This makes him difficult to place: a postmodernist who believes in depth, a genre filmmaker who destabilizes genre from within, an artist of the unconscious working in the most constructed of media.

(1980) The Elephant Man

FILM/DIRECTORS/Dino-Risi.title: Dino Risi
FILM/DIRECTORS/Dino-Risi.content:
Movement: Italian Post-Neorealism (adjacent)
Mode: Commedia all’italiana
Dino Risi made comedies that refused to stay comedies. Working in Italy’s popular cinema during the boom years of the economic miracle, he developed commedia all’italiana into a form capable of social diagnosis—films that made audiences laugh at themselves, then showed them what they were laughing at. His comedies are fast, sharp, and commercial; they’re also melancholic, critical, and occasionally devastating. The joke keeps going until it doesn’t.
Modernist problem
How can popular comedy carry the weight of cultural critique—diagnosing modernity’s anxieties without abandoning entertainment?
Risi’s answer: let the comedy run until it curdles. Don’t announce the darkness; embed it in structure, let it emerge through accumulation, then arrive without warning.
Commedia all’italiana
The genre Risi helped define uses comedy to examine Italian society during the postwar boom: consumer abundance, eroding traditions, new anxieties masked by prosperity. Unlike Hollywood comedy, commedia all’italiana doesn’t resolve its contradictions—it exposes them. Characters charm their way through chaos until the chaos wins. The laughter is real; so is the unease beneath it.
Risi’s protagonists are often men performing confidence they don’t feel, surviving on charm, momentum, and denial. They’re seductive and they’re doomed.
Form and tone
Risi’s films move fast—snappy dialogue, location shooting, episodic structures, improvisational energy. The pacing feels light until you notice what it’s evading. Tonal shifts arrive without preparation: a comic scene shades into melancholy; a road trip ends in death. The technique is to make the surface so enjoyable that the depths catch you off guard.
Speed and drift
In (1962) Il sorpasso, Risi’s masterpiece, the car becomes a philosophical object—mobility as compulsion, speed as avoidance. Bruno can’t slow down because slowing down means confronting everything the motion conceals. This is drift wearing vitality’s mask: forward movement that goes nowhere, charm that covers emptiness, comedy that’s also elegy.
Relation to Italian modernism
Risi operates adjacent to Antonioni and Fellini, diagnosing the same cultural moment through different means. Where they work in art cinema’s register—alienation made visible through architecture and silence—Risi works in popular forms, making alienation visible through its denial. His films are funnier than Antonioni’s and sharper than most comedies. They prove that critique doesn’t require difficulty.
Links
(1962) Il sorpasso
Existential drift
(1960) La dolce vita

FILM/DIRECTORS/Éric-Rohmer.title: Éric Rohmer
FILM/DIRECTORS/Éric-Rohmer.content:
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Cahiers du cinéma
Éric Rohmer is the New Wave’s quietest revolutionary. No jump cuts, no fourth-wall breaks, no visible style at all—just people talking in rooms, on beaches, in cafés, about what they want and what they think they should want. The drama is in the gap between the two. His films are comedies of self-deception, and they’re funnier and more devastating than they look.
Modernist problem
How can cinema represent ethical decision-making as it actually happens—not as principle applied but as desire rationalized?
Rohmer’s answer: a cinema of speech, hesitation, and retrospective justification. Modernism through transparency rather than rupture.
Position within the French New Wave
Rohmer was the oldest of the Cahiers critics and the last to make features. He edited the journal through its most influential years, championing classical Hollywood while his colleagues moved toward experimentation. His own films preserve that classicism—clear images, real locations, minimal editing—while redirecting it toward moral uncertainty.
His influences are literary: Stendhal, Jane Austen, Pascal. His characters think in sentences, and those sentences reveal more than they intend.
Form and style
Rohmer’s technique is deliberately invisible. Natural light, long takes, real apartments and beaches, actors who seem to be improvising even when they’re not. The camera observes without commenting. This restraint makes speech the action—you watch people talk, and the talk is where everything happens.
Spatial blocking matters enormously. In (1969) My Night at Maud’s, who sits where on the bed maps the entire moral drama. Distance, posture, the decision to move closer or pull away—these are Rohmer’s dramatic events.
The moral tales
The Six Moral Tales (1963–1972) established his method. Each film follows a man who’s committed (or thinks he’s committed) to one woman but finds himself tempted by another. The temptation is usually resisted—but the resistance is exposed as self-serving, fearful, or based on fantasy. Rohmer isn’t interested in whether his characters do the right thing. He’s interested in how they convince themselves they have.
Later cycles
Comedies and Proverbs (1981–1987) and Tales of the Four Seasons (1990–1998) extend the method to different registers. The characters are younger, the settings more varied, the moral stakes sometimes lighter. But the core remains: people talking themselves into and out of things, constructing narratives about who they are, and occasionally catching themselves in the act.
Chance and environment
Rohmer loves chance encounters, seasonal weather, the texture of a particular place. These aren’t backgrounds—they’re forces. A rainstorm changes everything. A beach vacation creates possibilities that wouldn’t exist elsewhere. His characters live in environments that shape their feelings without their noticing.
Legacy
Rohmer’s influence is vast and often unacknowledged. Richard Linklater’s talk-driven romances, Hong Sang-soo’s recursive relationship comedies, Greta Gerwig’s attention to young women’s self-narration, Mia Hansen-Løve’s naturalism—all descend from Rohmer. He proved that cinema could be philosophical without being difficult, that conversation could carry as much weight as action, that the quietest films could be the most revealing.
Links
Language and form as thought
Epistemological doubt
(1969) My Night at Maud’s

FILM/DIRECTORS/Federico-Fellini.title: Federico Fellini
FILM/DIRECTORS/Federico-Fellini.content:
Movement: Italian Post-Neorealism
Federico Fellini started as a neorealist screenwriter and ended as cinema’s great dreamer. The journey from Rome, Open City to 8½ traces his gradual turn inward—from documenting postwar Italy to constructing private mythologies of memory, fantasy, and spectacle. He kept the neorealist commitment to location shooting and ordinary faces, but filtered everything through imagination until reality became porous, invaded by dreams, hallucinations, and the ghosts of childhood. His cinema is excess where Antonioni’s is emptiness, warmth where Antonioni is chill, carnival where Antonioni is architecture.
Modernist problem
How can cinema represent inner life—memory, desire, creative paralysis, spiritual confusion—when psychological realism and linear narrative prove insufficient?
Fellini’s answer: dissolve the boundary. Let fantasy, autobiography, and spectacle become the film’s material, not its interruption.
From realism to myth
The early films—La Strada, Nights of Cabiria—retain neorealist ethics while introducing heightened emotion and symbolic figures. With (1960) La dolce vita and (1963) 8½, Fellini enters modernism decisively. Causal storytelling gives way to episodic structure; protagonists wander rather than act; the films become inquiries into identity and authorship rather than stories about characters.
Modern life isn’t explained anymore. It’s staged, remembered, reimagined.
Autobiographical modernism
Fellini treats his own memory and creative crises as formal engines. Guido in 8½, Marcello in La dolce vita—they’re fragmented self-portraits, caught between public roles and private disintegration. Childhood, sexuality, Catholic ritual, artistic failure recur not as confession but as mythic material. Life and art collapse into each other, and autobiography becomes method rather than claim to truth.
Spectacle and the carnivalesque
The mature style embraces excess: circuses, parades, religious processions, grotesque bodies, theatrical tableaux. These don’t offer escape—they structure meaning. Modern Italy appears as a space where sacred and profane coexist uneasily, where ritual has replaced psychology and visual density has replaced introspection.
The circus isn’t decoration. It’s Fellini’s model for what art is: chaotic, communal, performed, and without guarantees.
Time, memory, and drift
Narrative time in Fellini is elastic. Episodes accumulate rather than progress, producing drift and suspension. Memory operates not as flashback but as lived present—the past doesn’t explain the now, it coexists with it. The result is cinema of movement without destination, emotionally saturated but narratively open.
Relation to Italian modernism
If Antonioni externalizes alienation through architecture and silence, Fellini internalizes crisis through memory, fantasy, and overflow. Both dismantle classical narrative; Fellini replaces emptiness with abundance, abstraction with sensuality, restraint with emotional excess. His work defines a mythopoetic branch of Italian cinema, proving modernism can be warm.
Links
(1960) La dolce vita
(1963) 8½
Existential drift
Memory as structure in modernist cinema

FILM/DIRECTORS/François-Truffaut.title: François Truffaut
FILM/DIRECTORS/François-Truffaut.content:
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Cahiers du cinéma
François Truffaut is the New Wave’s warmest director, and maybe its most deceptive. The films feel accessible—classical storytelling, emotional clarity, characters you can care about—but underneath runs a modernist current: open endings, autobiographical self-exposure, the quiet refusal to moralize. He proved you could make personal, formally conscious cinema without alienating audiences.
Modernist problem
How can cinema remain emotionally direct while asserting personal authorship and formal independence?
Truffaut’s answer: ground modernism in intimacy rather than rupture. The experiments are there, but they serve feeling rather than demonstrating technique.
Position within the French New Wave
A founding Cahiers du cinéma critic and the movement’s most visible public figure. His 1954 essay “A Certain Tendency of the French Cinema” attacked the literary-psychological tradition of postwar French film, calling for a cinema of personal vision and lived experience—what became known as the politique des auteurs.
His influences split between Jean Renoir’s humanism and fluid camera, and Alfred Hitchcock’s narrative precision. Truffaut wrote the book on Hitchcock (literally), and his own films balance those two impulses: warmth and control.
Autobiography and authorship
Truffaut’s cinema is autobiographical without being confessional. (1959) The 400 Blows draws directly on his troubled childhood—reform school, absent parents, stealing—but transforms personal pain into something more universally legible. Antoine Doinel becomes Truffaut’s alter ego across five films, growing up on screen in a project that blurs fiction and memoir.
The autobiographical thread isn’t self-indulgence; it’s method. Personal experience becomes raw material for formal and emotional exploration.
Form and style
Truffaut retains narrative coherence while experimenting quietly with tone, structure, and point of view. Location shooting and naturalistic performance coexist with carefully shaped plots. The freeze-frame ending of The 400 Blows, the rapid montage of (1962) Jules et Jim—these are modernist gestures, but they don’t call attention to themselves. They serve the story.
His visual style is more classical than Godard’s or Resnais’s, but the openness is there: behavior without judgment, endings without resolution, emotion without explanation.
Humanist modernism
Truffaut’s films are preoccupied with childhood, education, literacy, desire, and the formation of self. They treat these as modernist problems—how do we become who we are?—without abandoning emotional accessibility. The tension in his work is usually between freedom and attachment, desire and responsibility.
Later films like The Soft Skin, Stolen Kisses, Day for Night, and The Story of Adele H. refine this approach: formally inventive, emotionally generous, resistant to moral closure.
Legacy
Truffaut demonstrated that authorship and narrative pleasure aren’t opposed. He helped institutionalize the director as an artistic figure, shaping arthouse culture, film education, and cinephilic discourse worldwide. His influence runs through Steven Spielberg, Martin Scorsese, Wes Anderson, Richard Linklater, and Greta Gerwig—filmmakers who make personal cinema within accessible forms.

French New Wave
Ellipsis and absence in modernist cinema

(1962) Jules et Jim

FILM/DIRECTORS/Ingmar-Bergman.title: Ingmar Bergman
FILM/DIRECTORS/Ingmar-Bergman.content:
Movement: Scandinavian Existential Cinema
Ingmar Bergman is cinema’s great interrogator of the soul—or what remains when the soul empties out. Working primarily in Sweden from the late 1940s through the early 1980s, he built a body of work obsessed with faith’s collapse, the cruelty of intimate relationships, the terror of death, and the fragile possibility of grace. His films are chamber dramas of the spirit: small casts, confined spaces, faces in extreme close-up, language pushed to its limits and then past them into silence. Where other modernists externalize alienation through architecture or society, Bergman turns the camera inward, making the psyche itself the landscape.
Modernist problem
How can cinema represent spiritual crisis—the loss of God, the failure of love, the confrontation with mortality—when interiority resists visualization and language fails?
Bergman’s answer: the face. Close-ups held so long they become landscapes. Two faces pressed together until identity blurs. The human countenance as site of revelation and concealment, the place where the soul would be visible if it existed.
The face
No filmmaker has used the close-up with such sustained intensity. Bergman’s collaborations with cinematographer Sven Nykvist produced some of cinema’s most extraordinary images of the human face: Liv Ullmann and Bibi Andersson merging in Persona, Harriet Andersson dying in Cries and Whispers, the children watching in Fanny and Alexander. The face becomes the arena where faith, doubt, cruelty, and tenderness fight for dominance. Dialogue matters less than what passes across features in silence.
God’s silence
Bergman grew up the son of a Lutheran minister, and the problem of faith—not its presence but its absence—structures his major work. The “trilogy of faith” (Through a Glass Darkly, Winter Light, The Silence) tracks the withdrawal of God from human experience. What replaces Him isn’t atheism but a void that still has His shape. Characters pray to silence, confess to emptiness, seek meaning in a universe that offers none. This is existential crisis as lived religious experience rather than philosophical position.
The cruelty of intimacy
Bergman’s couples wound each other with surgical precision. Marriage in his films is a theater of humiliation—partners who know exactly where to cut, who use intimacy as weapon. Scenes from a Marriage is the definitive statement: love as a decades-long negotiation between need and destruction. But even in his harshest work, Bergman allows for tenderness, for moments when connection briefly holds against the void.
Theater and cinema
Bergman ran theaters throughout his film career, and his cinema bears the mark: confined spaces, small ensembles, dialogue that feels written for the stage. But he’s also purely cinematic—the close-up, the dream sequence, the fluidity between interior and exterior reality. His films exist at the intersection of theatrical intensity and cinematic intimacy. The stage gives him structure; the camera gives him access to what actors cannot perform, only reveal.
Collaboration
Bergman worked repeatedly with the same actors (Liv Ullmann, Bibi Andersson, Max von Sydow, Harriet Andersson, Erland Josephson, Gunnar Björnstrand) and the same cinematographer (Sven Nykvist). This repertory company allowed him to develop a shared language, a shorthand of gesture and glance that deepens across films. Watching Bergman’s work chronologically means watching relationships between actors accrue meaning, faces age, partnerships evolve.
Late style
After the international breakthrough of The Seventh Seal and Wild Strawberries, Bergman’s work grew increasingly spare and interior. The color films of the 1970s (Cries and Whispers, Scenes from a Marriage, Autumn Sonata) strip away plot for psychological excavation. Then Fanny and Alexander arrives as summa: a return to childhood, to theater, to the full emotional range—cruelty and magic, death and resurrection, the family as prison and refuge. He called it his last film, and though he made more for television, it functions as a capstone.

(1957) Wild Strawberries
(1963) Winter Light
(1966) Persona
(1972) Cries and Whispers

FILM/DIRECTORS/Jacques-Demy.title: Jacques Demy
FILM/DIRECTORS/Jacques-Demy.content:
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Adjacent / Left Bank–adjacent
Jacques Demy believed the musical could tell the truth. Not despite the singing and the color-coordinated sets, but through them. His films are formally artificial—every umbrella matches, every line is sung—and emotionally devastating. He takes the Hollywood genres he loved (musicals, melodrama, fairy tales) and strips out the guaranteed happy endings, leaving the beauty intact but making it ache.
Modernist problem
How can classical forms—musicals, melodrama, romance—be renewed to express modern emotional life without collapsing into nostalgia or escapism?
Demy’s answer: stylization as emotional truth. The artifice doesn’t hide reality; it makes reality more visible.
Position within the French New Wave
Demy wasn’t a Cahiers critic. He was married to Agnès Varda and close to the Left Bank, but his project was different: not dismantling classical cinema but reinventing it from within. Where Godard destroys genre to expose its machinery, Demy inhabits genre to reveal its emotional possibilities.
His influences are Hollywood: Max Ophüls (camera movement, circularity), Vincente Minnelli (color as feeling), the MGM musical at its peak. But he uses these tools for stories without guaranteed resolution.
Form as feeling
In (1964) The Umbrellas of Cherbourg, every word is sung. In (1967) The Young Girls of Rochefort, the town itself is repainted to match the palette. These aren’t gimmicks. The continuous music makes every mundane exchange carry melodic weight; the coordinated colors externalize emotional states.
Color in Demy is structural. When Geneviève’s circumstances dim in Umbrellas, so does her environment. When Rochefort invites happiness, the whole town turns pastel. The visual design does the work that dialogue can’t.
Chance and constrained romance
Demy’s plots run on coincidence and near-miss. Characters meet, separate, almost-meet again. Love is real but fragile, shaped by forces larger than individual feeling: war, class, obligation, timing. In Umbrellas, the lovers are completely sincere—and completely thwarted. In Rochefort, everyone keeps searching for the person who might be around the next corner.
This is romantic modernism: love matters, but it doesn’t determine fate. Circumstance does.
Gender and labor
Beneath the luminous surfaces, Demy’s films take women seriously. Geneviève’s choice in Umbrellas isn’t failure—it’s survival under constraint. The twins in Rochefort have ambitions beyond romance. Even the fairy-tale heroines (Donkey Skin) navigate patriarchal structures with intelligence and resistance.
The critique is embedded in the melodrama, not announced. See Feminist frameworks in cinema.
The Demy universe
Characters recur across films. Lola from Lola (1961) reappears in Umbrellas. Roland Cassard, rejected in Lola, gets his happy ending years later. Demy builds a world where memory, fate, and loss circulate—Balzac’s Human Comedy rewritten as provincial French musical.
Legacy
Demy revitalized the musical as a form capable of emotional complexity. His influence runs through Wong Kar-wai, Xavier Dolan, Damien Chazelle (La La Land is explicitly indebted), and Greta Gerwig. Long overshadowed by more radical New Wave figures, he’s now recognized as essential—proof that beauty and sincerity can illuminate the structural limits of love.
Links
Color as meaning in modernist cinema
Feminist frameworks in cinema

(1967) The Young Girls of Rochefort

FILM/DIRECTORS/Jacques-Rivette.title: Jacques Rivette
FILM/DIRECTORS/Jacques-Rivette.content:
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Cahiers du cinéma
Jacques Rivette makes films that feel like they’re still being invented as you watch them. He came out of Cahiers with Godard and Truffaut, but his project was stranger: not fracturing narrative or reworking genre, but dissolving narrative authority altogether. His films are long—sometimes impossibly long—and they refuse to explain themselves. They treat cinema as a process of discovery rather than a delivery of meaning.
Modernist problem
How can cinema sustain investigation and play without collapsing into resolution or interpretive mastery?
Rivette’s answer: trust duration, trust actors, trust the viewer to stay curious without needing answers.
Position within the French New Wave
Rivette was part of the founding Cahiers group, but he was always the outlier. While Godard dismantled cinema from inside and Truffaut renovated it with warmth, Rivette built elaborate systems—conspiracies, mysteries, theatrical games—and then refused to solve them. His films ask questions for three, four, twelve hours, and end with the questions still hanging.
He’s closer to Borges than to Hitchcock: labyrinths you enter knowing there’s no exit.
Duration and process
Rivette embraces length as meaning. Out 1 runs nearly thirteen hours. Even his shorter films feel unhurried—scenes unfold in real time, actors improvise, the camera waits. This isn’t self-indulgence; it’s method. Duration lets process show. You see characters figuring things out, trying things, failing. The film becomes a record of its own making.
Improvisation and collaboration
Rivette co-creates with his actors. Scripts are frameworks; dialogue is often invented on set. Performers like Juliet Berto, Bulle Ogier, and Jean-Pierre Léaud become collaborators rather than interpreters. The line between character and performer blurs—you’re watching people play, and the play is the point.
Conspiracy and doubt
His films are full of secret societies, hidden codes, mysterious houses. But unlike thrillers, they don’t resolve. The conspiracy in Out 1 may be real or paranoid fantasy. The house in (1974) Céline and Julie Go Boating may be haunted or imagined. Rivette is interested in Epistemological doubt—in what it feels like to suspect there’s a pattern without ever confirming it.
Feminist modernism
Many of Rivette’s best films center women: their friendships, their creativity, their resistance to narrative punishment. Céline and Julie is about two women who rescue each other (and a fictional child) from a patriarchal melodrama. La Belle Noiseuse watches a woman refuse to be reduced to an image. His cinema gives women agency, curiosity, and the power to refuse the stories that would contain them. See Feminist frameworks in cinema.
Legacy
Rivette’s influence runs through filmmakers drawn to duration, ambiguity, and process: Chantal Akerman, Olivier Assayas, David Lynch, Apichatpong Weerasethakul, Miguel Gomes. He represents the New Wave’s most sustained challenge to the idea that films should explain themselves—and the most joyful argument that not knowing can be its own reward.
Links
Epistemological doubt
Active spectatorship in modernist cinema
Feminist frameworks in cinema
(1974) Céline and Julie Go Boating

FILM/DIRECTORS/Jean-Luc-Godard.title: Jean-Luc Godard
FILM/DIRECTORS/Jean-Luc-Godard.content:
Movement: French New Wave
Branch: Cahiers du cinéma
Jean-Luc Godard is the most radical figure of cinematic modernism. Where other New Wave directors broke with convention, Godard dismantled the medium itself—treating film as a tool for critical thought rather than storytelling, and subjecting every element (narrative, image, sound, genre) to interrogation.
Modernist problem
How can cinema reveal, rather than conceal, its own ideological construction?
Godard’s answer is perpetual disruption. Transparency is the enemy. Every technique that makes a film feel natural—continuity editing, psychological motivation, genre convention—is a technique that hides the work of ideology. So he breaks them open.
Position within the French New Wave
A founding Cahiers du cinéma critic, Godard began as a cinephile obsessed with Hollywood genre films. His early work (Breathless, Vivre sa vie, Contempt) reworks noir, melodrama, and the musical through New Wave spontaneity: handheld cameras, location shooting, jump cuts, direct address. Genre becomes raw material for quotation and critique rather than a structure to inhabit.
By the mid-1960s, the playfulness curdles. Pierrot le Fou and Weekend treat genre as exhausted, modern life as saturated with images that have replaced experience. The romantic outlaw, the couple on the run, the road movie—all exposed as dead forms, available only for autopsy.
Core formal strategies

Jump cuts and discontinuous editing that fracture classical time
Direct address and fourth-wall breaks
Sound–image disjunction that blocks emotional absorption
Quotation from literature, philosophy, advertising, other films
Genre borrowing and deconstruction
Characters as carriers of ideas rather than psychological subjects

Political modernism (mid–late 1960s)
After 1968, Godard abandons narrative cinema almost entirely. With Anne-Marie Miéville and the Dziga Vertov Group, he makes essayistic, didactic films shaped by Marxism and semiotics. Images become ideological units to be analyzed rather than consumed. The films are often difficult, sometimes unwatchable, always uncompromising.
Later work
From Sauve qui peut (la vie) (1980) onward, Godard’s cinema turns reflective and archival. Histoire(s) du cinéma reconceives film history as montage—fragments of old movies, photographs, music, and text layered into a meditation on what cinema was and what it failed to be.
Legacy
Godard’s influence extends across narrative film, essay film, political cinema, and video art. Filmmakers from Chantal Akerman and Abbas Kiarostami to Quentin Tarantino and Wong Kar-wai work in his wake. He is also a key figure in the prehistory of postmodern cinema—the meta-textuality, genre play, and image-saturation that define later filmmakers often trace back to his 1960s work.
Links
Self-reflexivity and anti-illusionism
Active spectatorship in modernist cinema
Language and form as thought
Color as meaning in modernist cinema

FILM/DIRECTORS/Jean-Pierre-Melville.title: Jean-Pierre Melville
FILM/DIRECTORS/Jean-Pierre-Melville.content:
Movement: Adjacent to French New Wave
Position: Precursor / Transitional figure
Jean-Pierre Melville is the New Wave’s godfather—older, cooler, already making films his own way while the Cahiers critics were still writing reviews. He built his own studio, shot on location before it was doctrine, and treated American genre cinema with the reverence the younger generation would later bring to Hawks and Hitchcock. He didn’t write manifestos. He made crime films where crime was almost beside the point.
Modernist problem
How can genre cinema be stripped to ritual, code, and gesture—eliminating psychology, explanation, and narrative reassurance?
Melville’s answer: reduction as method. His films get sparser and more abstract as they go, until action becomes ceremony and silence speaks louder than dialogue.
Position relative to the French New Wave
Melville isn’t a New Wave filmmaker in the strict sense—too old, too disciplined, too uninterested in rupture. But his influence on the movement is foundational. (1956) Bob le Flambeur directly shaped (1960) Breathless: the handheld night photography, the jazz atmosphere, the drifting protagonist defined by style rather than motive. Godard, Truffaut, and Chabrol learned from him that you could make films outside the studio system, that American genres could be raw material for European sensibilities.
Where the New Wave embraced spontaneity and fragmentation, Melville pursued austerity and control. His films feel inevitable where theirs feel contingent.
Form and style
Melville strips crime cinema to its skeleton. Sparse dialogue, long silences, geometric compositions. His actors don’t emote—they move through space with the precision of ritual. Urban settings become abstract: depopulated streets, anonymous apartments, spaces emptied of everything but function.
The violence is procedural rather than cathartic. Heists unfold as process. Shootouts happen quickly and without moral commentary. Death arrives as fact, not drama.
Code as meaning
In Melville’s underworld, loyalty and precision matter more than profit. His criminals follow self-imposed rules that grant dignity but also guarantee doom. Fidelity to code leads inevitably toward isolation or death—but the alternative is worse, because without the code there’s nothing at all.
This is existentialism filtered through genre. Honor becomes a fragile way of making meaning in a world that offers none.
The Resistance
Melville fought in the Resistance during World War II, and (1969) Army of Shadows brings that experience to the screen—not as heroic memoir but as procedural nightmare. The gangster codes of his crime films turn out to be rehearsals for this: a world where loyalty is life or death, where betrayal is inevitable, where killing your own people is sometimes the only option. The film strips war of glory the way his crime films strip heists of excitement. What remains is the terrible arithmetic of underground warfare.
Legacy
Melville bridges American noir and European modernism, proving that genre can function as philosophy. His influence runs through Michael Mann, John Woo, Wong Kar-wai, Jim Jarmusch, and the Coen brothers. The cool criminal, the ritualized heist, the silence that says more than speech—these are his inventions, still circulating.
Links
(1956) Bob le Flambeur
(1969) Army of Shadows
Existential drift
Chance and fate in modernist cinema

FILM/DIRECTORS/Michelangelo-Antonioni.title: Michelangelo Antonioni
FILM/DIRECTORS/Michelangelo-Antonioni.content:
Movement: Italian Post-Neorealism
Michelangelo Antonioni made films about people who can’t connect, and he made them by refusing to connect in all the ways cinema usually does. No explanatory dialogue, no resolved plots, no emotions announced through music or performance. Instead: architecture, silence, duration, and the spaces between people that never close. His protagonists wander through modernist landscapes—unfinished buildings, industrial zones, resort towns emptied of meaning—and the camera watches them with the same cool attention it gives to walls and water. Alienation isn’t his subject; it’s his method.
Modernist problem
How can cinema represent inner life when emotion no longer finds expression through action, dialogue, or narrative resolution?
Antonioni’s answer: displace meaning onto environment. What characters feel is visible in the spaces they occupy, the distances they maintain, the silences they can’t fill.
The alienation trilogy
The foundational statement: (1960) L’Avventura, (1961) La Notte, and (1962) L’Eclisse. Across these films, relationships form and dissolve without cause or resolution. Characters disappear—literally in L’Avventura, emotionally in La Notte, formally in L’Eclisse’s seven-minute coda that removes its protagonists entirely. Plot gives way to mood; architecture overtakes human drama; alienation becomes structure rather than theme.
Space as psychology
Antonioni’s environments aren’t backgrounds—they’re the films’ primary expressive material. Modern cities, construction sites, geometric interiors: these spaces diagram emotional states that characters can’t articulate. Architecture doesn’t reflect psychology; it replaces it. Where classical cinema locates meaning in motivation and dialogue, Antonioni locates it in framing, distance, and the textures of the visible world.
Form and style
Long takes that refuse to resolve. Ellipses that skip what other films would show. Minimal dialogue, restrained performances, careful geometric compositions. Ambient sound—wind, traffic, industrial noise—replacing score. These strategies produce a cinema of observation that requires active interpretation. The films don’t tell you what to feel; they show you what’s there and wait.
Time as drift
Antonioni rejects goal-directed time. His films unfold through duration rather than progression, producing suspension rather than development. Characters wander, wait, linger. Time feels emptied of purpose, unhooked from outcome. This is drift formalized—the temporal structure of a world where action no longer leads anywhere in particular.
Color as environment
(1964) Red Desert marks a rupture. Antonioni’s first color film doesn’t simply add color to his existing method — it transforms it. He physically painted the landscape: trees sprayed grey, grass dyed, smoke tinted. Color becomes phenomenological rather than representational — it doesn’t symbolize Giuliana’s states, it produces them. The industrial landscape of Ravenna shifts between toxic and sublime, and the shift is felt in the nervous system before it’s understood by the mind. Where the trilogy displaced meaning onto space, Red Desert displaces it onto the entire sensory field. It’s also his most compassionate film: Giuliana’s suffering is genuine illness, not the cool structural condition of the trilogy, and the camera registers her pain with an empathy Antonioni’s earlier work deliberately withheld.
The image as epistemology
(1966) Blow-Up marks another shift. Antonioni moves from Italy to London, from architecture to photography, from the question of whether emotion can find expression to the question of whether reality can find evidence. Thomas the photographer enlarges his images trying to prove a murder happened, and the more he enlarges, the less certain anything becomes — detail dissolves into grain, evidence into abstraction. When the photographs are stolen, the event itself disappears. The trilogy asked what remains when human connection fails; Blow-Up asks what remains when the image fails. It’s Antonioni’s first engagement with the image-saturated world of media, fashion, and surfaces — and his most explicit argument that reality is not captured by images but produced by them.
Relation to Italian modernism
Where Fellini internalizes crisis through memory and spectacle, Antonioni externalizes it through space and silence. Both dismantle classical narrative; Fellini fills the gap with excess, Antonioni with emptiness. Together they define Italian post-neorealism’s two poles: warmth and chill, carnival and architecture, overflow and restraint.
Links
(1960) L’Avventura
(1961) La Notte
(1962) L’Eclisse
(1964) Red Desert
(1966) Blow-Up

Photograph as death

Architecture as psychology in cinema

Active spectatorship in modernist cinema
