Core characteristics

Genre awareness: Films know what kind of films they are — and the audience knows too
Intertextuality: Quotation, homage, and recombination replace innovation from scratch
Media literacy as assumption: The spectator’s familiarity with genre conventions is prerequisite
Tone instability: Comedy and violence, sincerity and irony, coexist without resolution
Pop culture as texture: Music, brand names, TV, and consumer culture are part of the aesthetic fabric
Narrative complexity: Nonlinear structures, unreliable narration, nested realities

Key figures
David Lynch (dream logic, identity fracture, Americana as nightmare), Quentin Tarantino (genre recombination, dialogue as performance), the Coen Brothers (genre pastiche, entropy, dark comedy), Jim Jarmusch (minimalism, deadpan, cultural collision), Steven Soderbergh (formal restlessness, genre versatility), Charlie Kaufman (meta-narrative, authorship anxiety)
Relation to other movements
American Postmodern Cinema succeeds New Hollywood, which absorbed European modernism into American genre filmmaking. Where New Hollywood directors (Scorsese, Coppola, Altman) used modernist techniques to renovate classical forms — producing films that were both genre entertainment and personal expression — the postmodern generation inherits the renovation as a given. The question is no longer “how do we bring art cinema into Hollywood?” but “what do we do now that the distinction between art and genre has collapsed?”
The movement operates within the Postmodernist cinema paradigm while retaining connections to Modernist cinema — particularly in Lynch’s and Malick’s work, which preserves modernism’s commitment to interiority and duration within postmodern frameworks.
Links

David Lynch
(1977) Eraserhead
(1986) Blue Velvet
(1990) Twin Peaks
(1990) Wild at Heart
(1992) Twin Peaks - Fire Walk with Me
(1997) Lost Highway
(1999) The Straight Story
(2001) Mulholland Drive
(2006) Inland Empire
(2017) Twin Peaks - The Return
Postmodernist cinema
New Hollywood
Modernist cinema
Self-reflexivity and anti-illusionism
Identity as performance
Genre as raw material

FILM/MOVEMENTS/French-New-Wave.title: French New Wave
FILM/MOVEMENTS/French-New-Wave.content:
Scope:
c. 1959–1968, France
The French New Wave is cinema’s modernist revolution—the moment when a generation of critics-turned-filmmakers seized the means of production and remade the art form in their image. Emerging from the cinephile culture of Cahiers du cinéma and the parallel experiments of the Left Bank, the New Wave rejected the polished literary adaptations of the French “tradition of quality” in favor of personal expression, formal rupture, and the camera as pen. They shot on location with lightweight equipment, cast unknowns, embraced improvisation, and treated American genre cinema with the same seriousness the academy reserved for literature. The result was a cinema that felt spontaneous, self-aware, and radically present—films that knew they were films and reveled in the knowledge.
Historical context
The New Wave emerges from a specific cultural moment: postwar France, cinephilia as a way of life, the Cinémathèque Française as church and school. Young critics at Cahiers du cinéma—Godard, Truffaut, Rohmer, Rivette, Chabrol—spent their twenties watching everything, arguing about everything, and developing a theory of authorship (la politique des auteurs) that elevated the director to the status of artist. They championed Hollywood craftsmen (Hawks, Hitchcock, Ford) while attacking the prestige pictures of their own country as impersonal, script-bound, and cinematically dead.
When they finally made their own films, they did so with minimal budgets, real locations, and the freedom that comes from having nothing to lose. Lightweight cameras (the Arriflex, the Éclair) and portable sound equipment made it possible to shoot in apartments, on streets, in cafés—the texture of actual Paris rather than studio reconstructions. The films looked different because they were made differently.
The two currents
The New Wave is often treated as a single movement, but it contains at least two distinct tendencies that share techniques while pursuing different concerns.
Cahiers du cinéma — Jean-Luc Godard, François Truffaut, Éric Rohmer, Jacques Rivette, Claude Chabrol
The Cahiers directors came to cinema through cinephilia. They loved movies—American movies especially—and their films are saturated with references, homages, and quotations. Genre is raw material to be dismantled and reassembled. Godard’s gangsters quote Bogart; Truffaut’s criminals are tender and doomed; Rivette’s conspiracies refuse to resolve. The tone ranges from playful to melancholic, but the posture is consistent: cinema commenting on cinema, form made visible, the director’s personality stamped on every frame.
Left Bank — Alain Resnais, Agnès Varda, Chris Marker
The Left Bank filmmakers came to cinema through other arts—literature, photography, documentary—and their concerns are different: memory, time, history, politics, the essay form. Resnais collaborates with novelists (Duras, Robbe-Grillet) to create temporal labyrinths; Marker invents the essay film, mixing documentary and fiction, image and text; Varda develops a feminist cinema of everyday life and female subjectivity. Where the Cahiers directors foreground style and authorship, the Left Bank foregrounds consciousness and history. Both groups share formal innovations—location shooting, elliptical editing, reflexive address—but apply them to different ends.
Formal innovations
The New Wave didn’t invent these techniques, but it made them central to narrative cinema:


The jump cut — Godard’s signature: cutting within a continuous action, disrupting spatial and temporal continuity, making the edit visible. What classical cinema hides, the New Wave shows. The jump cut says: this is a film, time has been manipulated, pay attention.


Location shooting — Real apartments, real streets, real cafés. Paris becomes a character, and the films breathe with the texture of actual places. This isn’t just economy; it’s philosophy. The world presses in on the characters; they exist in a real city, not a constructed set.


Improvisation and spontaneity — Scripts were loose or absent; actors discovered dialogue on set; scenes were shot with the energy of discovery. The results feel alive in ways that polished productions don’t—rougher, more present, closer to how life actually unfolds.


Direct address — Characters look at the camera, speak to the audience, break the fourth wall. The classical cinema’s transparent window becomes a mirror, and the viewer’s position is made visible.


Quotation and reflexivity — Films reference other films, characters discuss cinema, posters and paintings appear in frame. The New Wave is cinema that knows its own history and makes that knowledge part of the experience.


The films
1959 is the breakthrough year: Truffaut’s The 400 Blows wins at Cannes, Resnais’s Hiroshima mon amour stuns audiences with its temporal complexity, and suddenly French cinema has a movement. Over the next several years, the New Wave produces an extraordinary concentration of major work:

(1959) The 400 Blows — Truffaut’s autobiographical debut, the freeze-frame ending
(1959) Hiroshima mon amour — Resnais and Duras, memory as structure
(1960) Breathless — Godard’s manifesto, the jump cut, Michel’s Bogart
(1961) Last Year at Marienbad — Resnais’s labyrinth, time without ground
(1962) Cléo from 5 to 7 — Varda’s real-time female subjectivity
(1962) Jules et Jim — Truffaut’s doomed triangle, Catherine’s smile
(1962) Vivre sa vie — Godard’s portrait of Nana, Dreyer’s Falconetti
(1962) La Jetée — Marker’s photo-roman, memory and death
(1963) Contempt — Godard’s essay on cinema itself
(1964) The Umbrellas of Cherbourg — Demy’s sung-through musical, color as emotion
(1965) Pierrot le Fou — Godard’s explosion, the escape that fails
(1966) Masculin Féminin — Godard’s sociology of youth
(1969) My Night at Maud’s — Rohmer’s moral tale, talk as action

Fragmentation and aftermath
By the mid-1960s, the movement fragments. Godard moves toward increasingly radical political cinema, eventually abandoning narrative entirely. Truffaut settles into craftsmanship and classical storytelling. Rohmer pursues his moral tales with quiet persistence. Rivette extends duration toward the epic. Varda develops feminist documentary. Marker becomes uncategorizable. The unity was always partly a critical construction; what the filmmakers shared was a moment, not a program.
May 1968 accelerates the dissolution. Godard and others attempt to shut down the Cannes festival; political commitment seems to demand the rejection of bourgeois art cinema; the playful modernism of the early sixties suddenly looks frivolous. Some directors radicalize; others retreat into more traditional forms; the New Wave as a unified movement ends.
But its principles diffuse globally. New Hollywood, New German Cinema, the Brazilian Cinema Novo—all inherit New Wave techniques and attitudes. Richard Lester’s A Hard Day’s Night (1964) represents the most improbable diffusion: New Wave form entering mass popular culture through a Beatles comedy, jump cuts and handheld cameras reaching fifty million teenagers who had never heard of Cahiers. The idea that cinema can be personal, that the director is an author, that form can be self-conscious without being cold—these become permanent features of art cinema. The New Wave made modernism popular, and the world’s cinema has never recovered.
Links

Jean-Luc Godard
François Truffaut
Agnès Varda
Alain Resnais
Chris Marker
Jacques Demy
Jacques Rivette
Éric Rohmer
Ellipsis and absence in modernist cinema
Self-reflexivity and anti-illusionism
Existential drift

FILM/MOVEMENTS/Italian-Neorealism.title: Italian Neorealism
FILM/MOVEMENTS/Italian-Neorealism.content:
Scope:
c. 1943–1952, Italy
Italian Neorealism emerges during and immediately after World War II as cinema’s ethical turn toward material reality, everyday life, and social precarity. Against the white-telephone fantasies of Fascist-era Italian cinema, neorealist filmmakers took cameras into the streets—shooting on location amid rubble and poverty, casting nonprofessional actors, refusing the consolations of plot resolution. The movement treated cinema not as entertainment or propaganda but as moral witness: a way of attending to lives that official culture had ignored or aestheticized away.
Historical context
Neorealism arises from specific material conditions: the collapse of Cinecittà’s studio infrastructure during the war, the availability of lightweight cameras and portable equipment, and the urgent need to document a society in ruins. But the movement is also an ethical response to Fascism’s lies—its glossy surfaces, heroic narratives, and denial of ordinary suffering. Neorealism insists on showing what was hidden.
The movement’s theoretical foundation comes partly from critics like Cesare Zavattini, who argued for a cinema of “following” rather than constructing—tracking people through their days rather than imposing dramatic arcs. André Bazin later championed neorealism as cinema’s truest vocation: respecting the ontological integrity of reality rather than subordinating it to narrative or symbol.
Core formal principles

Location shooting — real streets, apartments, countryside rather than studio sets
Nonprofessional actors — faces that carry history rather than performance training
Ordinary protagonists — workers, unemployed, children, the poor
Open-ended narratives — stories that refuse tidy resolution
Duration and observation — scenes that linger on process, waiting, movement without payoff
Contingency — events shaped by social forces and chance rather than individual will

Ethical orientation
Neorealism treats cinema as moral encounter. The camera’s attention to faces, gestures, and everyday labor is itself an ethical act—a refusal to look away from precarity, to simplify suffering into melodrama, or to offer false consolation. The movement rejects the idea that cinema’s job is to reassure. Instead, it asks audiences to witness lives structured by poverty, injustice, and limited agency. (See Neorealism as an ethical project)
This ethical commitment shapes form: the refusal of closure means audiences leave without catharsis; the use of real locations means the world presses in on characters rather than serving as backdrop; the casting of nonprofessionals means performance carries the weight of actual circumstance.
Key figures and films
Roberto Rossellini — Rome, Open City (1945), Paisà (1946), Germany Year Zero (1948)
The foundational trilogy. Rossellini shoots in the ruins of war, mixing documentary and fiction, refusing to separate the two. Rome, Open City was made while Germans still occupied northern Italy; the film’s rough texture comes partly from necessity, partly from ethical choice.
Vittorio De Sica — Shoeshine (1946), Bicycle Thieves (1948), Umberto D. (1952)
De Sica and screenwriter Zavattini pursue neorealism’s logic most rigorously. Bicycle Thieves follows an unemployed man and his son through Rome searching for a stolen bicycle—a plot that refuses heroism, refuses resolution, refuses to let the protagonist escape his circumstances. Umberto D. attends to an elderly man and his dog with such patience that Zavattini called it an attempt to film “ninety minutes of a man’s life to whom nothing happens.”
Luchino Visconti — La Terra Trema (1948)
Visconti shoots entirely on location in a Sicilian fishing village, casting local fishermen, using their dialect. The film applies neorealist principles to class analysis, showing how economic structures trap communities across generations.
Contingency and everyday life
Neorealism’s most radical gesture is its treatment of time. Classical cinema subordinates duration to drama—every scene advances plot. Neorealism allows scenes to continue past their narrative function: people walking, waiting, performing small tasks. This isn’t padding; it’s philosophy. By showing time that doesn’t “pay off,” neorealism insists that life exceeds story, that people exist beyond their usefulness to narrative.
This commitment to contingency—to what happens rather than what should happen—anticipates modernist cinema’s later experiments with duration and drift.
Decline and transformation
By the early 1950s, neorealism’s moment passes. Italian audiences, exhausted by poverty, turn toward entertainment; government censorship pressures filmmakers away from social critique; the movement’s major figures evolve in different directions. Rossellini moves toward spiritual inquiry; Visconti toward operatic melodrama and historical spectacle; De Sica toward international co-productions.
But neorealism doesn’t disappear—it transforms. Federico Fellini and Michelangelo Antonioni, both of whom worked on neorealist productions, carry its commitment to location shooting and everyday texture into new territory: subjective interiority, existential crisis, modernist form. Italian Post-Neorealism inherits neorealism’s methods while redirecting them from social documentation toward psychological and spiritual exploration.
Legacy
Neorealism establishes the ethical and formal foundations for European art cinema. Its influence extends through the French New Wave (which admired its production methods and moral seriousness), global new wave movements, and contemporary realist cinema. The movement proves that cinema can be a form of attention—a way of seeing that is itself a moral act.
Links

Neorealism as an ethical project
Italian Post-Neorealism
Modernist cinema
Active spectatorship in modernist cinema

FILM/MOVEMENTS/Italian-Post-Neorealism.title: Italian Post-Neorealism
FILM/MOVEMENTS/Italian-Post-Neorealism.content:
Scope:
c. 1958–1968, Italy
Italian Post-Neorealism marks the decisive shift from the social, material focus of Italian Neorealism toward a modernist cinema of interiority, alienation, and existential crisis. As Italy undergoes rapid economic and cultural transformation—the miracolo economico—filmmakers such as Federico Fellini, Michelangelo Antonioni, and Pier Paolo Pasolini reorient realism away from collective social conditions and toward subjective experience, desire, memory, and spiritual exhaustion.
Rather than abandoning neorealism, post-neorealist cinema transforms it. Location shooting, everyday environments, and non-spectacular life remain central, but are repurposed to map psychological states onto cities, landscapes, and bodies. Realism becomes inward-facing: a tool for probing consciousness rather than documenting social reality. The result is one of the foundational strands of European art cinema, shaping modernist approaches to narrative, time, and subjectivity.
Historical context
By the mid-1950s, classical neorealism had exhausted itself. Italian audiences, tired of poverty on screen after years of poverty in life, turned toward entertainment. Government censorship pressured filmmakers away from social critique. The economic miracle brought prosperity, consumer culture, and a new set of anxieties that the older realist forms couldn’t address.
The filmmakers who emerged in this moment—Fellini and Antonioni had both worked on neorealist productions—inherited neorealism’s techniques while redirecting them toward new concerns. The question was no longer “how do the poor survive?” but “why does prosperity feel empty?” The social gave way to the existential; the collective to the individual; documentation to diagnosis.
Two poles: Fellini and Antonioni
Post-neorealism divides into two dominant modes, often seen as complementary opposites:
Antonioni’s architecture of alienation — Antonioni externalizes inner emptiness through space. His characters drift through modern environments—unfinished buildings, resort towns, geometric interiors—that dwarf and absorb them. Dialogue fails; relationships dissolve without cause; the camera lingers on empty frames after characters exit. Alienation becomes formal: not a theme to be discussed but a structure to be inhabited. The “alienation trilogy” ((1960) L’Avventura, (1961) La Notte, (1962) L’Eclisse) defines this mode.
Fellini’s carnival of memory — Fellini internalizes crisis through fantasy, spectacle, and autobiography. His characters inhabit worlds saturated with circus imagery, religious procession, and theatrical excess. Memory and dream invade the present; the boundary between inner and outer dissolves. Where Antonioni empties, Fellini overflows. (1960) La dolce vita and (1963) 8½ define this mode—the former diagnosing modern Rome as spectacular emptiness, the latter turning creative paralysis into cinematic form.
Core characteristics
Real space as psychological environment — Cities, beaches, apartments, and modern architecture function as expressions of alienation, desire, or spiritual crisis rather than neutral backdrops. Space mirrors interior states; the EUR district in L’Eclisse is as much a character as Vittoria.
Fragmented or episodic narrative — Stories unfold through chapters, ellipses, and drifting sequences rather than tight causal progression. La dolce vita’s seven nights, 8½’s nested memories and fantasies—these structures reflect unstable identities and emotional disorientation.
Ambiguity over resolution — Films reject moral clarity and conventional closure. Endings don’t resolve; they suspend. The famous final shots—Marcello turning away from the girl on the beach, Guido joining the circus procession, Vittoria and Piero simply not appearing—leave meaning open rather than settled.
Time as drift — Duration and slowness express existential suspension rather than progress. Characters wander instead of acting; time feels emptied of direction or purpose. This is Existential drift made formal—the temporal structure of lives that have lost their narrative.
The economic miracle as spiritual crisis
Post-neorealism’s historical moment is the miracolo economico: rapid industrialization, new prosperity, consumer culture, the transformation of Italian society from rural and traditional to urban and modern. The films respond to this moment not with celebration but with diagnosis. Abundance produces loneliness; mobility produces drift; modernization produces loss of meaning.
Marcello in La dolce vita moves through a Rome of parties, celebrities, and spectacle, present everywhere, committed nowhere. Guido in 8½ has success, money, a film to make—and can’t make it, can’t want anything clearly, can’t escape the ghosts of his past. The economic miracle gave Italians everything except reasons to live.
Timeline

Mid-1950s: Classical neorealism declines; Fellini’s early work (La Strada, Nights of Cabiria) introduces heightened emotion and spiritual themes within realist frameworks
1960: La dolce vita and L’Avventura mark the decisive break—international sensations that announce a new kind of cinema
Early 1960s: The movement peaks with Antonioni’s trilogy, Fellini’s 8½, Pasolini’s poetic-political work
Mid-1960s: Diversification—Fellini toward autobiography and fantasy, Antonioni toward color and international production, Pasolini toward myth and provocation
Post-1968: The movement dissolves into political radicalism, genre experimentation, and new popular forms

Legacy
Italian Post-Neorealism proves that realist techniques can serve modernist ends—that location shooting and attention to everyday texture can map inner life as effectively as social conditions. Its influence runs through European art cinema and beyond: the French New Wave admired and borrowed from it; later filmmakers from Wim Wenders to Sofia Coppola inherit its attention to space, drift, and beautiful emptiness.
The movement also establishes the two great modes of cinematic modernism: Antonioni’s cold, architectural, subtractive approach and Fellini’s warm, carnivalesque, additive one. Between them, they define the poles within which art cinema still operates.
Links

Federico Fellini
Michelangelo Antonioni
Italian Neorealism
Existential drift

FILM/MOVEMENTS/Japanese-Golden-Age-Cinem.title: Japanese Golden Age Cinema
FILM/MOVEMENTS/Japanese-Golden-Age-Cinem.content:
Scope:
c. 1935–1965, Japan (peak: late 1940s–early 1960s)
Japanese cinema’s golden age produced two of the medium’s most influential and antithetical sensibilities: Akira Kurosawa’s kinetic, humanist, action-driven filmmaking and Yasujirō Ozu’s still, contemplative, formally rigorous domestic dramas. That these two directors emerged from the same national cinema at the same historical moment — postwar Japan, caught between tradition and modernity, devastation and reconstruction — tells you something about the range of responses art can have to the same crisis.
Historical context
Japanese cinema had been developing since the silent era, with its own conventions: the benshi (live narrator), a slower editing pace than Hollywood, and a studio system organized around genre categories (jidaigeki for period films, gendaigeki for contemporary dramas). By the 1930s, directors like Ozu, Mizoguchi, and Naruse had established a sophisticated art cinema operating within commercial studio constraints.
The war disrupted everything. The postwar period — American occupation (1945–52), economic reconstruction, rapid modernization — produced a cinema shaped by two pressures: the need to reckon with destruction and defeat, and the influx of Western (especially American) cinematic influence. Kurosawa absorbed Hollywood’s storytelling energy and redirected it through Japanese themes and formal ambition. Ozu deepened his commitment to stillness and domestic ritual, creating films that seemed to resist modernity’s acceleration by slowing down.
The international breakthrough came at Venice: Kurosawa’s (1950) Rashomon won the Golden Lion in 1951, introducing Western audiences to Japanese cinema virtually overnight. Through the 1950s, Kurosawa, Ozu, Mizoguchi, and others produced an extraordinary concentration of major work, much of it reaching international festivals. By the early 1960s, a younger generation (Oshima, Imamura, Teshigahara) was emerging with more radical formal and political ambitions — the Japanese New Wave — and the golden age was giving way to something harder and stranger.
Two poles
Kurosawa: movement, action, humanism. Kurosawa is the most Deleuzian movement-image director in Japanese cinema. His films are driven by physical action, moral choice, and kinetic editing. The samurai film becomes a vehicle for ethical philosophy; the crime thriller becomes a study of social structure. He absorbed Hollywood’s narrative energy (he admired John Ford) and combined it with a visual ambition — widescreen compositions, weather as dramatic force, deep staging — that exceeded anything Hollywood was doing. His humanist commitments are direct: his films believe that individuals can act, that choices matter, that courage and compassion are real.
Ozu: stillness, duration, acceptance. Ozu is the opposite impulse. His films depict the cycles of domestic life — marriage, aging, loss, seasonal change — with a formal rigor that borders on the ritualistic. The camera sits at tatami level, rarely moves, holds compositions long past narrative necessity. Pillow shots (empty corridors, trains passing, a vase) punctuate the drama like breathing. Schrader identifies Ozu as one of the three foundational practitioners of transcendental style. Where Kurosawa’s cinema is about what people do, Ozu’s is about what people endure — and the grace that endurance can produce.
Other key figures
Kenji Mizoguchi (long takes, female suffering, historical drama), Mikio Naruse (quiet domestic tragedy), Kon Ichikawa (The Burmese Harp, Fires on the Plain — war’s aftermath), Masaki Kobayashi (Harakiri, Kwaidan — tradition as cruelty). Each extends the range, but the Kurosawa/Ozu axis defines the movement’s gravitational poles.
Relation to other movements and paradigms
Japanese Golden Age Cinema operates within Modernist cinema but with a different relationship to classical form than the European movements. The French New Wave defined itself against a tradition of quality; Japanese directors worked within their studio system, pushing its boundaries rather than breaking them. Kurosawa’s genre films (samurai, noir, adventure) parallel Classical Hollywood cinema’s commitment to storytelling while exceeding its formal ambition. Ozu’s domestic dramas seem almost anti-cinematic in their stillness — the furthest thing from Hollywood — and yet they were commercially successful in Japan for decades.
The movement’s influence on Western cinema is enormous. Kurosawa directly inspired Hollywood (Seven Samurai → The Magnificent Seven, Yojimbo → A Fistful of Dollars, The Hidden Fortress → Star Wars). Ozu’s influence is slower and deeper: through Schrader’s writing, through Wim Wenders’s documentary Tokyo-Ga, and through the global slow cinema movement that would emerge decades later.
Links

Akira Kurosawa
Yasujirō Ozu

Classical Hollywood cinema
Movement-image vs time-image
Transcendental style and slow cinema

FILM/MOVEMENTS/New-Hollywood.title: New Hollywood
FILM/MOVEMENTS/New-Hollywood.content:
Scope:
c. 1967–1980, United States
New Hollywood refers to the generation of American filmmakers who, inspired by European modernism — particularly the French New Wave — and enabled by the collapse of the studio system’s Production Code, brought personal authorship, formal experimentation, and moral ambiguity into mainstream American cinema. For roughly a decade, directors wielded unprecedented creative control, producing genre films that doubled as art cinema: crime films without moral clarity, road movies without destinations, war films without heroism.
Historical context
The studio system’s decline through the 1960s — falling audiences, the end of the Production Code, the demographic shift toward younger viewers — created a vacuum that a generation of film-school-educated directors filled. They had watched Godard and Fellini and Bergman and Kurosawa, and they wanted to make American films with that ambition and freedom. The studios, desperate and disoriented, let them.
The key year is 1967: Bonnie and Clyde and The Graduate prove that American audiences will accept moral ambiguity, open endings, and European-inflected style. By the early 1970s, the movement is in full bloom.
Core characteristics

Auteur-driven: The director as creative authority, not the studio
Moral ambiguity: Antiheroes, unresolved endings, ethical complexity
Location shooting and naturalistic performance
European formal influence: Jump cuts, long takes, elliptical editing, direct address
Genre renovation: Crime, western, war, and melodrama stripped of classical reassurance
Social engagement: Vietnam, Watergate, civil rights, counterculture

Key figures
Martin Scorsese, Francis Ford Coppola, Robert Altman, Arthur Penn, Hal Ashby, Terrence Malick (early work), Brian De Palma, Peter Bogdanovich, William Friedkin
Relation to other movements
New Hollywood absorbs French New Wave techniques and Modernist cinema’s seriousness within the framework of American genre filmmaking. It inherits Classical Hollywood cinema’s production infrastructure while rejecting its moral certainties and invisible style. It anticipates American Postmodern Cinema but maintains a distinction the next generation would collapse: the belief that personal artistic vision can renovate genre from within, rather than treating genre as material for quotation or play.
The movement ends roughly with the commercial disasters of the late 1970s — Heaven’s Gate (1980) being the symbolic endpoint — when studios reasserted control and the blockbuster economy took hold.
Legacy
New Hollywood proved that European art cinema’s innovations could work within American commercial frameworks, creating a model of the director-as-auteur that persists in independent and mainstream cinema. Its influence on American Postmodern Cinema is direct: the next generation inherited its ambitions while operating in a cultural landscape where the earnest belief in art’s transformative power had been complicated by irony, media saturation, and the failure of 1960s idealism.
Links

French New Wave
Classical Hollywood cinema
Modernist cinema
American Postmodern Cinema
Postmodernist cinema

FILM/MOVEMENTS/Scandinavian-Existential-.title: Scandinavian Existential Cinema
FILM/MOVEMENTS/Scandinavian-Existential-.content:
Scope:
c. 1950–1982, Sweden (primarily), Denmark, and broader Nordic region
Scandinavian Existential Cinema—centered on the work of Ingmar Bergman but extending to Carl Theodor Dreyer’s late films and other Nordic filmmakers—represents one of modernist cinema’s most sustained engagements with questions of faith, mortality, and the soul. Where Italian Post-Neorealism externalizes alienation through architecture and urban space, Scandinavian existentialism turns inward, using the face, the chamber drama, and the confined domestic space to explore spiritual crisis, the failure of intimacy, and the confrontation with death.
Historical and cultural context
The movement emerges from Protestant Northern Europe—specifically Lutheran Sweden and Denmark—where questions of faith, guilt, and individual conscience carry particular cultural weight. Bergman, the son of a minister, grew up inside the church’s structures and spent his career interrogating what remained when belief collapsed. The long Nordic winters, the austerity of Protestant aesthetics, and the tradition of introspective literature (Strindberg, Ibsen, Kierkegaard) all inform the movement’s concerns.
Unlike the French New Wave’s urban energy or Italian modernism’s engagement with postwar capitalism, Scandinavian existential cinema is often rural, interior, and focused on small groups—families, couples, isolated individuals—rather than society at large.
Core concerns

The silence of God — faith’s absence as presence; the void that still has God’s shape
Death and mortality — not as plot event but as condition, as the fact that organizes consciousness
The failure of intimacy — couples who wound each other, families who cannot comfort, touch that doesn’t reach
The face — the human countenance as the site where the soul would be visible if it existed; close-ups held until they become landscapes
Performance and authenticity — theater as metaphor for selfhood; the question of whether any presentation of self is true
Guilt and confession — Protestant conscience turned inward, seeking absolution that doesn’t come

Formal characteristics

Chamber drama structure: small casts, confined spaces, intense focus
Extreme close-ups, particularly of faces
Black-and-white photography (Bergman’s 1950s-60s work) or highly symbolic color (the red of Cries and Whispers, the contrasting palettes of Fanny and Alexander)
Theatrical intensity combined with cinematic intimacy
Dreams, visions, and memories integrated without explanatory transitions
Silence as active force—what isn’t said often matters more than dialogue

Relation to European modernism
Scandinavian existentialism shares modernist cinema’s rejection of classical narrative transparency and its commitment to subjective experience over plot. But where Antonioni empties space and Godard fragments form, Bergman intensifies presence—faces fill the frame, emotions run to extremes, spiritual questions are posed directly rather than diffused into style.
The movement’s literary antecedents (Strindberg, Kierkegaard) distinguish it from the cinephile culture of the French New Wave. Bergman came from theater, not from the Cinémathèque; his modernism is rooted in dramatic tradition rather than in a desire to reinvent cinema itself.
Key figures
Ingmar Bergman — The movement’s central figure. From The Seventh Seal (1957) and Wild Strawberries (1957) through Persona (1966), Cries and Whispers (1972), and Fanny and Alexander (1982), Bergman built cinema’s most sustained exploration of faith, death, and the soul. His repertory company of actors and his collaboration with cinematographer Sven Nykvist produced some of the most extraordinary images of the human face ever filmed.
Carl Theodor Dreyer — The Danish master whose late films (Ordet, 1955; Gertrud, 1964) share Bergman’s spiritual concerns while pursuing an even more austere formal approach. Dreyer’s influence on Bergman is direct; both filmmakers treat cinema as a space for metaphysical inquiry.
Timeline

1940s–50s: Bergman’s early films develop his theatrical style; Dreyer’s Ordet (1955) provides a model for cinematic metaphysics
1957: The Seventh Seal and Wild Strawberries establish Bergman internationally; the “faith trilogy” begins
1960s: Peak period of formal experimentation—Persona (1966) pushes the chamber drama to its limit
1970s: Color films (Cries and Whispers, Scenes from a Marriage) deepen psychological excavation
1982: Fanny and Alexander functions as summation and farewell

Legacy
Scandinavian existentialism demonstrates that cinema can engage directly with questions of faith, death, and the soul without sacrificing formal sophistication. Bergman’s influence extends through art cinema globally—from Woody Allen to Lars von Trier to Andrei Tarkovsky—proving that the face in close-up can carry philosophical weight, and that the chamber drama can be as formally radical as any experiment.

FILM/MOVEMENTS/Soviet-Poetic-Cinema.title: Soviet Poetic Cinema
FILM/MOVEMENTS/Soviet-Poetic-Cinema.content:
Scope:
c. 1960–1986, Soviet Union (primarily Russia, Ukraine, Georgia, Armenia)
Soviet Poetic Cinema names a loose tendency within post-Stalinist Soviet filmmaking: directors who rejected Socialist Realism’s demands for clarity, optimism, and ideological instruction in favor of lyrical, ambiguous, formally experimental work. Working within a state-controlled system that alternately tolerated and persecuted them, these filmmakers developed a cinema of elemental imagery, extended duration, and spiritual or philosophical inquiry. The movement’s central figure is Andrei Tarkovsky, but it extends across Soviet national cinemas—Sergei Parajanov’s Armenian and Ukrainian films, Tengiz Abuladze’s Georgian work, Aleksandr Sokurov’s late-Soviet experiments.
Historical context
The Khrushchev Thaw (1956–1964) loosened Soviet cultural controls enough for a new generation of filmmakers to emerge. They came of age after Stalin, studied at VGIK (the state film school), and inherited both Soviet montage theory (Eisenstein, Vertov) and exposure to international art cinema. The state supported film production generously—crews, equipment, time—while also censoring, delaying, and sometimes suppressing the results.
This contradiction produced a distinctive situation: filmmakers with resources to realize ambitious visions, constrained by ideological demands they couldn’t openly refuse. The result was often allegory, ambiguity, and formal density—films that could slip past censors by being difficult to pin down.
Against Socialist Realism
Socialist Realism demanded positive heroes, clear moral frameworks, collective purpose, and narratives that affirmed Soviet ideology. Poetic cinema offered none of this. Its protagonists were troubled, isolated, spiritually searching. Its narratives were fragmented or circular. Its imagery was dense, symbolic, resistant to single interpretation. Its relationship to Soviet history was melancholic or critical rather than triumphant.
The filmmakers didn’t directly attack the state—that would have been impossible—but they created spaces where Soviet ideology simply didn’t apply. This was their politics: not dissent but withdrawal into aesthetic and spiritual realms the state couldn’t fully control.
Core characteristics

Extended duration — long takes that let time pass without narrative justification, demanding contemplative attention
Elemental imagery — water, fire, earth, wind as recurring visual motifs with symbolic but irreducible force
Spiritual inquiry — questions of faith, transcendence, and meaning pursued seriously within an officially atheist state
Memory and time — non-linear structures where past and present interpenetrate
Ambiguity — refusal of clear interpretation, endings that don’t resolve
National and folk traditions — engagement with pre-Soviet cultural memory, religious iconography, regional aesthetics
The face and the landscape — close attention to human features and natural environments as carriers of meaning

Key figures
Andrei Tarkovsky — The movement’s central figure and most internationally recognized. His seven features—from Ivan’s Childhood (1962) through The Sacrifice (1986)—define poetic cinema’s possibilities: memory as form (Mirror), spiritual allegory (Stalker, Andrei Rublev), elemental imagery saturating every frame. Tarkovsky eventually went into exile, dying in Paris.
Sergei Parajanov — Armenian-Ukrainian director whose Shadows of Forgotten Ancestors (1965) and The Color of Pomegranates (1969) draw on folk traditions, religious iconography, and non-narrative visual poetry. Parajanov was imprisoned for years on fabricated charges; his work is among the most visually radical in cinema.
Aleksandr Sokurov — A younger figure who emerged in the late Soviet period, influenced by Tarkovsky but developing his own approach to duration, history, and the image. His work continues into the post-Soviet era.
Tengiz Abuladze — Georgian director whose Repentance (1984) became a landmark of glasnost-era cinema, addressing Stalinist terror through allegory.
Relation to European modernism
Soviet Poetic Cinema shares concerns with European art cinema—subjective time, formal experimentation, rejection of classical narrative—but develops them differently. Where Italian Post-Neorealism externalizes alienation through urban space and the French New Wave fragments through editing and quotation, Soviet poetic filmmakers pursue elemental imagery and extended duration. Their modernism is less urban, less ironic, more connected to landscape, folk tradition, and (covertly) religious sensibility.
Tarkovsky admired Bergman and the French New Wave but insisted on cinema’s unique capacity to “sculpt in time”—a claim for duration over montage that distinguished his approach from both Eisensteinian tradition and Western modernism.
The Zone as metaphor
Tarkovsky’s Zone in Stalker—a forbidden area where normal laws don’t apply, where wishes might be granted, where the seeker must approach with humility—functions as a metaphor for what poetic cinema itself sought: a space outside ideology, outside the state’s demands, where spiritual and aesthetic inquiry could proceed on its own terms. The Zone is dangerous, the authorities forbid entry, but it’s the only place where meaning might still be possible.
Legacy
Soviet Poetic Cinema proved that formally radical, spiritually serious filmmaking could emerge from within a repressive system—not by directly opposing it, but by creating aesthetic spaces it couldn’t fully comprehend or control. Tarkovsky’s influence extends globally: Béla Tarr, Terrence Malick, Theo Angelopoulos, Carlos Reygadas, and others inherit his commitment to duration, elemental imagery, and cinema as contemplative practice.
The movement also preserves pre-Soviet cultural memory—Orthodox Christianity, folk traditions, national identities—that the state sought to suppress or subsume. In this sense, poetic cinema was always quietly political, even when it claimed to be purely aesthetic.
Links

Andrei Tarkovsky
(1975) Mirror
(1979) Stalker
Memory as structure in modernist cinema
Modernist cinema

FILM/PARADIGMS/Classical-Hollywood-cinem.title: Classical Hollywood cinema
FILM/PARADIGMS/Classical-Hollywood-cinem.content:
Period: Roughly 1927–1960 (sound era through studio system collapse)
Center: Hollywood studio system
Relation to: Precedes Modernist cinema, provides the norms that Postmodernist cinema both inherits and ironizes
Classical Hollywood cinema refers to the dominant mode of American filmmaking from the coming of sound through the collapse of the studio system. It is less a style than a set of assumptions so thoroughly naturalized they became invisible: that stories proceed through cause and effect, that characters have legible psychologies, that editing should be seamless, that the camera shows objective reality, and that endings resolve. These assumptions are not neutral — they constitute an ideology of transparency, a claim that the world is knowable, moral, and narratable.
The system was industrial as well as aesthetic. Studios controlled production, distribution, and exhibition. Directors, writers, and actors worked under contract. Genre categories (western, musical, noir, melodrama, screwball comedy) organized production and audience expectation. Within these constraints, individual talents — Hawks, Hitchcock, Ford, Welles, Sirk — pushed against the norms without breaking them, producing work that is formally sophisticated precisely because it operates within and against a visible system.
Core characteristics

Continuity editing: The 180-degree rule, shot/reverse-shot, match cuts — techniques that create seamless spatial and temporal flow, hiding the apparatus behind the story
Narrative causality: Events linked by cause and effect, driven by character goals. Every scene advances the plot; nothing is gratuitous or contingent
Psychological realism: Characters with legible motivations, coherent interiority, and emotional arcs that the audience can follow and identify with
Invisible style: Cinematography and editing that serve the story without calling attention to themselves. The camera is a window, not a presence
Closure: Endings that resolve narrative questions and restore moral order. Problems are solved; couples unite; justice is served
Genre as contract: Genre categories establish expectations that the film fulfills, modifies, or (occasionally) subverts — but always acknowledges

Why it matters for this vault
Understanding classical cinema is essential for understanding everything else in this vault. Every formal strategy in Modernist cinema — the jump cut, the ambiguous ending, the unmotivated camera movement, the character who drifts rather than acts — gains meaning in relation to what it disrupts. Postmodernist cinema inherits classical conventions as raw material for quotation and play. New Hollywood renovates classical genre from within. Without the norms, the departures are invisible.

FILM/PARADIGMS/Modernist-cinema.title: Modernist cinema
FILM/PARADIGMS/Modernist-cinema.content:
Period: Roughly 1945–1980
Center: Europe (with global influence)
Relation to: Succeeds Classical Hollywood cinema, precedes Postmodernist cinema
Modernist cinema emerges after World War II as a sustained rejection of classical transparency. Where Hollywood’s studio system perfected an “invisible” style—continuity editing, psychological motivation, narrative resolution—European filmmakers shattered these conventions to produce cinema that foregrounds subjectivity, fragmentation, and epistemological uncertainty. The result is film as a mode of inquiry rather than storytelling, asking questions about consciousness, time, and meaning that classical cinema assumed were settled.
The trauma of the war is foundational. The old narratives—progress, moral clarity, rational order—had failed catastrophically. Modernist cinema registers this failure formally: plots that refuse causality, characters who drift rather than act, endings that withhold resolution. If classical cinema offered reassurance through narrative mastery, modernist cinema offers only the integrity of doubt.
What modernism rejects
Classical Hollywood cinema operates on assumptions so naturalized they become invisible: that stories proceed through cause and effect, that characters have legible psychologies driving their actions, that editing should be seamless, that the camera shows objective reality, that endings resolve. Modernist cinema makes these assumptions visible by violating them.
The rejection isn’t merely formal—it’s philosophical. Continuity editing implies a stable, knowable world. Narrative resolution implies moral order. Psychological realism implies that consciousness is transparent. Modernism disputes all of this: the world is fragmented, meaning is provisional, and interiority resists representation.
Core formal principles
Modernist cinema coheres around recurring strategies, each a response to classical norms:


Fragmented narrative: Plots unfold through ellipsis, gaps, and discontinuity rather than causal chains. Events may repeat, contradict, or remain unexplained. The viewer must actively construct coherence—or accept its absence.


Subjective time: Chronology bends toward interior states. Duration reflects perception, not clocks. Memory intrudes on the present; past and future collapse into simultaneity. Flashbacks become unreliable; the distinction between remembering and imagining blurs.


Ambiguity over closure: Endings resist resolution. Questions remain open. Meaning stays perspectival rather than authoritative. The viewer leaves uncertain what happened, or what it meant—and that uncertainty is the point.


Consciousness as subject: Interior states replace goal-driven action as the primary engine. Characters are defined by what they perceive, remember, and fail to understand rather than what they want and pursue. Modernist alienation becomes a formal problem, not just a theme.


Self-reflexivity and anti-illusionism: Films expose their own construction—direct address, visible apparatus, disrupted illusion. The goal is active spectatorship: viewers who think about what they’re watching rather than losing themselves in it.


Language and form as thought: Dialogue, narration, and visual form function as philosophical inquiry. Color, composition, and editing carry meaning that exceeds or contradicts speech. Cinema becomes capable of ideas, not just stories.


Historical and intellectual context
Modernist cinema develops in dialogue with literary modernism (Proust, Woolf, Joyce) and continental philosophy (Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, phenomenology, existentialism). The interest in subjective time, unreliable consciousness, and the limits of representation connects film to broader currents in twentieth-century thought.
The postwar moment matters. Italian Neorealism emerges from the rubble of fascism; the French New Wave rebels against the “Tradition of Quality”; Bergman interrogates faith’s collapse in secular Sweden; Tarkovsky works within and against Soviet constraints. Each context shapes a distinct modernism, but all share the sense that old forms cannot address new realities.
Movements
Modernist cinema isn’t monolithic—it comprises distinct movements with different emphases:


Italian Neorealism (1943–1952): The ethical foundation. Location shooting, non-professional actors, attention to poverty and ordinary life. Less formally radical than later movements, but establishes the break with studio artifice.


French New Wave (1958–1967): The most influential movement. Jump cuts, location shooting, genre play, direct address. Splits between the playful cinephilia of the Cahiers group and the political rigor of the Left Bank.


Italian Post-Neorealism (1960–1975): Antonioni’s architectural alienation, Fellini’s autobiographical excess. Modernism turned inward, toward psychology and memory.


Scandinavian Existential Cinema (1950s–1980s): Bergman’s chamber dramas of faith and intimacy. The face as landscape, silence as content.


Soviet Poetic Cinema (1960s–1980s): Tarkovsky and Paradjanov. Spiritual longing, material texture, time as sculptural medium.


Key figures
Jean-Luc Godard (radical deconstruction), Alain Resnais (memory and trauma), Michelangelo Antonioni (alienation and architecture), Ingmar Bergman (faith and interiority), Andrei Tarkovsky (spirituality and duration), Agnès Varda (feminist subjectivity), Federico Fellini (autobiography and spectacle)
The spectator’s role
Classical cinema positions the viewer as passive recipient—the apparatus hides itself, the story carries you along, meaning arrives pre-digested. Modernist cinema demands active spectatorship: interpretation, tolerance for ambiguity, willingness to sit with uncertainty. The film doesn’t tell you what to think; it creates conditions for thinking.
This is both democratic and demanding. The viewer becomes a collaborator in meaning-making rather than a consumer of finished product. But it also risks hermeticism—films that speak only to initiates, that mistake difficulty for depth.
Relation to other paradigms
Modernism defines itself against Classical Hollywood cinema—the invisible style, narrative resolution, psychological transparency. But it also anticipates Postmodernist cinema, which inherits modernism’s self-reflexivity while abandoning its seriousness. Where modernism seeks depth beneath surface, postmodernism questions whether depth exists at all. Where modernism treats ambiguity as epistemological problem, postmodernism treats it as occasion for play.
The boundary is porous. Late Godard, Rivette’s labyrinthine narratives, Fellini’s circus of surfaces—these sit at the threshold, modernist in origin but pointing toward something else.
Links
Modernism as a mode of inquiry
Movement-image vs time-image
Epistemological doubt
Existential drift

FILM/PARADIGMS/Postmodernist-cinema.title: Postmodernist cinema
FILM/PARADIGMS/Postmodernist-cinema.content:
Period: Roughly 1980–2000s (with precursors in the late 1960s)
Center: Global, but especially American independent and European art cinema
Relation to: Succeeds Modernist cinema, emerges alongside media saturation and poststructuralist theory
Postmodernist cinema extends modernism’s self-reflexivity while abandoning its faith in depth. Where modernist filmmakers shattered classical conventions to access something truer—subjective experience, the texture of memory, the weight of alienation—postmodernist cinema questions whether “something truer” exists at all. Meaning becomes play. Originality gives way to quotation. The image refers not to reality but to other images.
This isn’t simply cynicism. Postmodernism registers a genuine shift in how culture operates. By the 1980s, media saturation had changed the relationship between images and reality. Baudrillard’s “simulacrum”—the copy without an original—describes a world where representations circulate endlessly, detached from any referent. Cinema, already an art of reproduction, becomes the ideal medium for exploring this condition.
What postmodernism inherits and transforms
Postmodernism doesn’t reject modernism so much as radicalize certain tendencies while inverting others:

































ModernismPostmodernismDepth beneath surfaceSurface is the contentEpistemological doubtSkepticism about meaning itselfSelf-reflexivity as critiqueSelf-reflexivity as playAmbiguity as problemAmbiguity as pleasureOriginality through ruptureQuotation and recombinationHigh/low distinctionCollapse of hierarchy
The modernist asks: How can we know? The postmodernist asks: Does it matter?
Core formal principles
Postmodernist cinema coheres around strategies that treat meaning as constructed, contingent, and available for play:


Irony and genre awareness: Films undercut themselves, commenting on their own conventions while deploying them. Genre becomes material to manipulate rather than form to inhabit. The gangster film knows it’s a gangster film—and so does the audience.


Pastiche and intertextuality: Cultural fragments are recombined without the modernist anxiety about originality. Quotation replaces creation. Films are made of other films, and this isn’t a problem to solve but a condition to exploit.


Surface over depth: Style, attitude, and aesthetic pleasure replace psychological excavation. Characters become types, archetypes, or walking quotations. Interiority—modernism’s great subject—recedes.


Instability of meaning: Interpretation stays contingent, multiple, or deliberately unresolvable. But where modernist ambiguity produces anxiety, postmodernist ambiguity produces pleasure—the freedom to play rather than the burden of uncertainty.


Collapse of high/low: Pop culture and philosophy coexist without hierarchy. A film can be simultaneously trash and art, commercial and critical, entertaining and knowing. The distinction between serious and frivolous loses force.


Media reflexivity: Cinema acknowledges its own mediation—not to critique illusion (the modernist move) but to revel in the image-world we inhabit. Television, advertising, music video, and film blur together.


Historical and intellectual context
Postmodernism emerges alongside poststructuralist theory: Barthes’s death of the author, Derrida’s deconstruction, Foucault’s critique of grand narratives, Baudrillard’s simulation. These thinkers questioned the stable foundations—authorial intention, fixed meaning, objective truth—that even modernism had partially preserved.
The political backdrop matters too. The failures of 1968, Watergate, the exhaustion of revolutionary hope, the rise of consumer capitalism and media culture—all contribute to a sensibility that treats earnestness with suspicion. If modernism was shaped by the trauma of world war, postmodernism is shaped by the banality of spectacle.
Precursors within modernism
The transition isn’t clean. Several filmmakers working within modernist movements anticipate postmodern strategies:


Jean-Luc Godard: By Weekend (1967), Godard treats narrative as exhausted, genre as dead form. His work after 1968 becomes increasingly essayistic and citational—modernist in rigor, postmodern in its skepticism toward representation.


Jacques Rivette: The labyrinthine conspiracies, the films-within-films, the sense that narrative is a game to be played rather than a truth to be revealed.


Jacques Demy: The musical’s artifice mixed with genuine emotion—a genre simultaneously inhabited and held at a distance.


Chris Marker: Documentary, fiction, and essay collapse into hybrid forms that question the authority of any single mode.


Key figures
David Lynch (dream logic, Americana as nightmare), Quentin Tarantino (genre recombination, dialogue as performance), the Coen Brothers (genre pastiche, ironic distance), Pedro Almodóvar (melodrama reclaimed), Wong Kar-wai (style as substance), Wes Anderson (aesthetic as worldview), Charlie Kaufman (meta-narrative, authorship anxiety)
Movements and tendencies
Postmodernism is less a movement than a condition—it permeates rather than coheres. But certain tendencies cluster:


American Postmodern Cinema (1980s–1990s): The Coens, Tarantino, Soderbergh, Jarmusch. Genre awareness, pop-culture dialogue, ironic distance from classical form.


New Queer Cinema (early 1990s): Identity as performance, genre subversion, camp aesthetics reclaimed.


Late European art cinema: Almodóvar, von Trier (Dogme 95’s ironic sincerity), Haneke (media critique that implicates the viewer).


Hong Kong Second Wave: Wong Kar-wai, Tsui Hark. Style as content, genre play, postmodern cool.


The spectator’s role
Postmodernist cinema assumes a media-literate viewer—someone who gets the references, recognizes the quotations, and enjoys the play of surfaces. This can produce pleasure: the satisfaction of recognition, the fun of genre games, the freedom from earnestness. It can also produce exhaustion: the sense that nothing means anything, that irony has become compulsory, that sincerity is unavailable.
The best postmodernist cinema holds these in tension—ironic and sincere, playful and serious, surface and (somehow) depth.
Critiques and limits
Postmodernism faces charges of political emptiness (play without stakes), cultural elitism (requiring extensive media literacy), and emotional thinness (irony as defense against feeling). These critiques aren’t always fair, but they identify real tensions.
By the 2000s, a “post-postmodernism” or “New Sincerity” emerges—filmmakers trying to recover earnestness without naivety, to make films that care about something. Whether this marks postmodernism’s exhaustion or its next phase remains unclear.
Relation to other paradigms
Postmodernism inherits Modernist cinema’s self-reflexivity while rejecting its faith in depth. It anticipates contemporary digital cinema’s further erosion of distinctions between original and copy, real and virtual. The relationship is dialectical: postmodernism couldn’t exist without modernism’s ruptures, and whatever comes next will likely be shaped by postmodernism’s insights and limitations.
Links
Modernist cinema
American Postmodern Cinema
Self-reflexivity and anti-illusionism
Identity as performance
Genre as raw material
Jean-Luc Godard
Jacques Rivette
David Lynch
(1997) Lost Highway

FILM/SOURCES/Lynch-—-Catching-the-Big-Fi.title: Lynch — Catching the Big Fish
FILM/SOURCES/Lynch-—-Catching-the-Big-Fi.content:
Author: David Lynch
Title: Catching the Big Fish: Meditation, Consciousness, and Creativity
Year: 2006
Type: Book (short essays/reflections)
Lynch’s creative manifesto, structured as a collection of brief, aphoristic reflections on filmmaking, Transcendental Meditation, and the relationship between consciousness and art. The book’s central metaphor: ideas are fish. Small fish swim near the surface. The big ones — the ones that change everything — live in the deep. Meditation doesn’t catch ideas; it expands the ocean you’re fishing in. You surface refreshed, with a sharpened intuition, and then you go catch ideas.
This is the key to understanding why Lynch’s films resist intellectual interpretation. He doesn’t construct puzzles or embed hidden meanings. He dives into the unconscious and brings back what he finds there, presenting it with the conviction of a dream — not decoded, not rationalized, just given. The formal strategies of his films (dream logic, substitution, the double, sonic dread) aren’t techniques applied to content; they’re the natural shape of material retrieved from below the surface of conscious thought.
On the filmmaker
The art life: Lynch describes the conditions creativity requires — unstructured time, freedom from obligation, a container large enough for unexpected things to surface. “Art life means a freedom to have time for the good things to happen.” This explains his working method: extended production schedules, willingness to follow impulses, resistance to pre-planned narrative.
TM as creative practice: Lynch has practiced Transcendental Meditation since 1973. He doesn’t use it to find ideas — this is a common misunderstanding. Meditation expands the container. During TM, the practitioner transcends ordinary thought to access what the Vedic tradition calls pure consciousness — an unbounded field beneath the surface activity of the mind. Lynch draws a parallel to the Upanishads: through meditation one realizes the unbounded; that which is unbounded is happy; there is no happiness in the small. The practice enlivens the ocean of consciousness and sharpens intuition. You come out refreshed and raring to go catch ideas. The fishing happens after.
Writing things down: “Write things down because if you forget it you’ll wanna kill yourself.” Ideas arrive as fragments — images, sounds, feelings — and if you don’t capture them immediately, they dissolve. Lynch’s notebooks and sketches are the infrastructure of his creative process. The films are assembled from these caught fragments.
“Where the attention is, that becomes lively”: Lynch’s version of the observer effect. The rooms we enter carry the residue of what happened in them. The things we attend to become more vivid. This principle operates in his filmmaking: the sustained attention of the camera on a dark hallway, a flickering lamp, a face — these become lively through sheer duration and focus.
Lost Highway and the OJ trial: Lynch has said Lost Highway is related to the OJ Simpson case — specifically the concept of psychogenic fugue, a psychological state in which a person creates an alternate identity to escape unbearable reality. Fred Madison, unable to face that he murdered his wife, becomes someone else. The film’s structure literalizes a dissociative break.
Key concepts introduced
Expanding the container: The central metaphor. Not finding bigger fish but deepening the water. This maps onto Lynch’s formal evolution: from Eraserhead’s claustrophobic industrial nightmare to Inland Empire’s vast, fractured, DV-shot expanse. The container gets larger as Lynch goes deeper.
Consciousness as ocean: Lynch describes pure consciousness as an ocean of creativity, intelligence, and bliss — with individual awareness as waves on the surface. This is Vedic cosmology (Brahman, the Upanishads, Advaita Vedanta) filtered through Maharishi Mahesh Yogi’s TM framework. The parallel to cinema is real: Schrader’s description of slow cinema’s mandala direction — cinema approaching meditation, duration invoking the contemplative — describes from the outside what Lynch describes from the inside. The filmmaker dives down; the slow cinema viewer is invited to do the same. See Transcendental style and slow cinema.
Desire as bait: Ideas come when you want them badly enough. Desire for an idea functions like bait on a hook — it draws the fish upward. But you can’t force it. You can only prepare the conditions (expand the container, sharpen the intuition) and then want.
Music marries the picture: Lynch insists on the unity of sound and image. His collaborations with Badalamenti aren’t scoring in the traditional sense — they’re the creation of sonic environments that exist inseparably from the visual. This connects to the Sound and darkness section of the David Lynch director note.
Connections

David Lynch — the filmmaker’s own account of his process. Everything in the director note about dream logic, genre as psychic architecture, and sound should be read alongside this.
Transcendental style and slow cinema — Lynch’s TM practice and Schrader’s transcendental style converge: both describe cinema (or consciousness) moving from surface activity toward stillness, from narrative toward contemplation. Lynch approaches it from inside (the meditator’s experience); Schrader maps it from outside (the critic’s taxonomy).
(1997) Lost Highway — the psychogenic fugue connection. Fred’s inability to face his crime generates the film’s bifurcated structure.
Identity as performance — Lynch’s framework complicates this: identity in his films isn’t performed so much as generated by the unconscious. The double isn’t a mask; it’s what the psyche produces when the original self can’t hold.
Architecture as psychology in cinema — Lynch’s “where the attention is, that becomes lively” is a theory of cinematic space: rooms carry psychic residue, and the camera’s attention activates it.

FILM/SOURCES/Schrader-—-Rethinking-Trans.title: Schrader — Rethinking Transcendental Style
FILM/SOURCES/Schrader-—-Rethinking-Trans.content:
Author: Paul Schrader
Title: “Rethinking Transcendental Style”
Year: 2018 (introduction to new edition of Transcendental Style in Film, originally 1972)
Type: Essay
Schrader revisits the framework he proposed at age 24: that certain filmmakers (Ozu, Bresson, Dreyer) shared a formal approach — transcendental style — that used cinematic means to evoke spiritual experience. The convergence was in style, not content. Not films about religion, but films whose formal strategies (delayed edits, withholding music, heightening the mundane) created a specific perceptual sequence: the everyday → disparity → decisive moment → stasis → transcendence.
Forty-five years later, Schrader reframes this within a larger map. Deleuze’s distinction between movement-image and time-image provides the theoretical backbone: postwar cinema shifted from action-driven editing to time-driven editing, from cuts motivated by on-screen movement to cuts motivated by associations across duration. Schrader argues that transcendental style was a way station in a larger movement away from narrative — a movement that has since exploded into what we now call slow cinema.
Andrei Tarkovsky is the fulcrum. Where Ozu and Bresson moved toward resolution through slowness, Tarkovsky brought resolution to slowness — extending delayed cuts into dead time, turning pillow shots into entire scenes. His international success made slow cinema fashionable and opened the door for Béla Tarr, Chantal Akerman, Tsai Ming-liang, and the global slow cinema movement that followed.
Key concepts introduced
The three-part sequence of transcendental style: everyday (the mundane depicted with care) → disparity (a growing crack in the surface, unease created by withholding) → decisive moment → stasis (acceptance, a parallel reality). The mechanics are formal: delay edits, avoid music cues, hold on empty spaces, let the spectator’s unease build until it resolves into something beyond narrative.
Slow cinema as three directions: When cinema breaks free from the narrative nucleus, it moves in one of three directions — (1) the surveillance camera (toward quotidian reality: neorealism’s endpoint, Akerman, Wang Bing, observational documentary), (2) the art gallery (toward pure light and color: experimental film, Brakhage, structural cinema, the mandala of abstract imagery), or (3) the mandala (toward meditation: durational cinema, Kiarostami’s Five, contemplative stillness as endpoint). Transcendental style proper sits just inside the “Tarkovsky Ring” that separates theatrical cinema from these three outer directions.
The delayed cut vs. the smash cut: Both manipulate time, but in opposite directions. The smash cut jumps ahead of expectation (action before you’re ready). The delayed cut holds past expectation — the character exits, the camera stays on the empty doorway for five beats. The smash cut depreciates the viewer’s participation; the delayed cut demands it. In dead time, the spectator is left to think or reflect. This connects directly to Active spectatorship in modernist cinema.
Duration as the key: What unites all slow cinema is time. Not slowness as mood but duration as formal principle. The long take is the sine qua non — not virtuosic tracking shots but static frames held longer than narrative requires, creating dissonance between the time a shot “should” last and the time it actually occupies.
Schrader’s diagram: A visual map placing filmmakers on a field radiating outward from narrative (“N”) at the center, through the Tarkovsky Ring, toward the three non-narrative endpoints. David Lynch appears near the center, close to narrative. Yasujirō Ozu and Robert Bresson sit just inside the ring. Tarkovsky straddles the ring itself. The diagram is subjective but useful for mapping relationships.
Connections

Movement-image vs time-image — Schrader’s entire framework rests on Deleuze’s periodization. The shift from movement-image to time-image is the precondition for everything he describes.
Andrei Tarkovsky — the fulcrum figure. Already in the vault; Schrader’s essay deepens the case for Tarkovsky’s pivotal role.
Robert Bresson — one of the three original transcendental style directors. Already in the vault as a director note.
Yasujirō Ozu — the other primary exemplar. Schrader identifies Ozu’s pillow shots and offset edits as prototypical transcendental style techniques.
Active spectatorship in modernist cinema — slow cinema’s central demand: the viewer must participate, not surrender.
Neorealism as an ethical project — one of transcendental style’s precursors. Bazin’s advocacy of the long take and the “ontology of the photographic image” feeds directly into this lineage.
Transcendental style and slow cinema — the IDEAS note where these concepts are developed as arguments about how cinema approaches the spiritual through form.
David Lynch — appears on Schrader’s diagram. Lynch’s relationship to transcendental style is oblique but real, especially in (1999) The Straight Story.

MUSIC/ARTISTS/Bob-Dylan.title: Bob Dylan
MUSIC/ARTISTS/Bob-Dylan.content:
Genres: folk rock, contemporary folk, singer-songwriter, blues rock, country rock
Scene: Greenwich Village folk scene
Era: 1961–present (primary period: 1962–1976)
RYM · Apple Music · Spotify
Bob Dylan arrived in New York in January 1961 as a nineteen-year-old from Hibbing, Minnesota, carrying an acoustic guitar and an encyclopedic knowledge of American folk, blues, and country music. Within five years he had rewritten the rules governing what a popular song could say and who was expected to say it. The scale of the disruption is measurable in the responses it provoked: The Beatles changed their songwriting, The Rolling Stones started writing their own material with new seriousness, the Brill Building model lost its monopoly on pop lyric craft, and an entire generation of musicians concluded that self-authored lyrics were essential. The songwriter-performer divide and the ideology of authenticity that reshaped popular music in the late 1960s are both, in part, consequences of what Dylan did between 1963 and 1966.
Influences and inheritance
Dylan’s education was the American vernacular. Woody Guthrie was the origin point — Dylan visited Guthrie at Greystone Park Hospital in Morris Plains, New Jersey, within days of arriving in New York, and wrote “Song to Woody” (1962) almost immediately afterward. What Dylan took from Guthrie was not a musical style but a model of what a folk singer could be: uncompromising, wielding the song as a tool of witness. Hank Williams gave him an understanding of how a song’s architecture could carry emotional weight independently of its words, a lesson audible in the spare, descending structures of The Times They Are a-Changin’ (1964). Robert Johnson and the Delta blues tradition shaped his vocal phrasing, the way he bends syllables against the grain of the melody. Little Richard, whom Dylan covered as a teenager in his Hibbing band the Golden Chords, gave him something the folk tradition lacked: rhythmic abandon, the understanding that a voice could be an instrument of physical force.
Greenwich Village in 1961 was the laboratory. The folk revival had concentrated a generation of musicians, scholars, and activists in a few square blocks of lower Manhattan, and the scene’s coffee houses (the Gaslight, Gerde’s Folk City, the Bitter End) functioned as both performance spaces and informal conservatories. Dylan absorbed material voraciously: Appalachian ballads, work songs, talking blues, the topical songwriting of Pete Seeger and the Almanac Singers. John Hammond signed him to Columbia Records on October 26, 1961 — the same A&R man who had signed Billie Holiday, Count Basie, and would later sign Bruce Springsteen. Columbia executives called Dylan “Hammond’s Folly.” His self-titled debut, released March 19, 1962, sold poorly. The Freewheelin’ Bob Dylan (1963), released May 27, 1963, changed everything.
Core musical identity
The voice is the first problem. It should not work. Thin and nasal, with a phrasing style that treats the melody as a suggestion rather than an obligation, Dylan’s singing violates every convention of mid-century popular vocal performance. What it does instead is foreground the words. Every syllable is articulated with a precision that compensates for the roughness of the instrument delivering it, and the deliberate avoidance of conventional beauty forces the listener into the language rather than the sound. Dylan’s vocal style is a rhetorical strategy: by refusing to sing prettily, he makes the lyrics impossible to ignore.
The songwriting changed what lyrics could do. Before Dylan, the pop lyric operated within inherited conventions: romantic love, heartbreak, teenage experience, rendered in accessible language and compressed into the verse-chorus framework. Dylan’s lyrics imported the resources of modernist poetry — surrealist imagery, stream-of-consciousness juxtaposition, allusion, ambiguity — without abandoning the song’s emotional directness. “A Hard Rain’s a-Gonna Fall” (1963) stacks apocalyptic images into a folk ballad structure borrowed from “Lord Randall”. “Subterranean Homesick Blues” (1965) delivers Beat poetry at the speed of a Chuck Berry single. “Desolation Row” (1965) builds an eleven-minute narrative populated by figures from literature and history without ever losing melodic coherence. No one in popular music had attempted this density of language, and the achievement created an expectation that lyrics should carry intellectual and literary weight, one that reshaped how rock music was written about and evaluated.
The restlessness is equally defining. Dylan has never occupied one musical identity for long. The acoustic protest singer of 1963 became the electric rock poet of 1965, who became the country minimalist of 1967, who became the born-again Christian of 1979, who became the blues traditionalist of the late 1990s. Each transformation alienated part of his audience and attracted another. The pattern is not arbitrary — it reflects a genuine resistance to being contained by any single genre, audience, or set of expectations. The electric performance at the Newport Folk Festival on July 25, 1965, when he took the stage with a band and played “Maggie’s Farm” (1965) and “Like a Rolling Stone” (1965) to a divided crowd, remains one of the defining moments of 1960s popular music precisely because it made the refusal to repeat himself visible and public.
Key records

The Freewheelin’ Bob Dylan (1963) — The arrival: “Blowin’ in the Wind” (1963), “A Hard Rain’s a-Gonna Fall” (1963), “Don’t Think Twice, It’s All Right” (1963). Topical songwriting elevated to poetry within the folk tradition
The Times They Are a-Changin’ (1964) — The political album: spare, acoustic, deliberately austere. The title track became an anthem; the album is starker and less varied than Freewheelin’
Another Side of Bob Dylan (1964) — The pivot away from protest: more personal, more playful, “My Back Pages” (1964) as the explicit rejection of his own public role
Bringing It All Back Home (1965) — Side one electric, side two acoustic; “Subterranean Homesick Blues” as the opening statement of intent, “Mr. Tambourine Man” and “It’s Alright, Ma (I’m Only Bleeding)” as proof that the acoustic mode could still carry weight
Highway 61 Revisited (1965) — The full electric commitment. “Like a Rolling Stone” (1965), released as a single on July 20, 1965, ran over six minutes, reached number two on the Billboard Hot 100, and redefined what a rock single could be. Al Kooper’s organ, Mike Bloomfield’s guitar, the band locked into a groove that never lets up
Blonde on Blonde (1966) — Recorded largely in Nashville with session musicians including Charlie McCoy, Kenny Buttrey, and Joe South alongside Kooper and Robbie Robertson, this is the sound of a rock artist working with Nashville’s craft tradition. “Visions of Johanna” (1966), “Sad Eyed Lady of the Lowlands” (1966), a double album that sustains its hallucinatory mood across seventy-three minutes
John Wesley Harding (1967) — After the motorcycle accident on July 29, 1966, Dylan retreated to Woodstock and emerged with an album of spare, biblical parables. Country instrumentation, no electric excess, the voice quieter and more deliberate
Blood on the Tracks (1975) — The return: a divorce album with the narrative compression of short fiction. “Tangled Up in Blue” (1975), “Idiot Wind” (1975), “Shelter from the Storm” (1975)

Legacy and influence
The influence operated through specific encounters as much as through records. The meeting with The Beatles at the Delmonico Hotel on August 28, 1964 — where Dylan, by multiple accounts, introduced them to marijuana — preceded the introspective turn that produced Rubber Soul and Revolver. Joe Strummer said Dylan “invented the whole field we all work in.” The singer-songwriter tradition (Leonard Cohen, Joni Mitchell, Paul Simon, Neil Young) is a direct inheritance, each of those artists working in a space that did not exist before Dylan demonstrated that lyrics could carry the weight of literature. Rock criticism as a discipline — the idea that popular music warranted sustained analytical attention — emerged partly because Dylan’s lyrics demanded it.
His influence on punk is less often acknowledged but equally direct: the stance of refusal, the insistence that authenticity means following your own impulse rather than meeting your audience’s expectations, the willingness to alienate. John Lydon said he liked Dylan specifically “when he went electric.” The punk ethos of constant reinvention against audience comfort is Dylan’s Newport moment generalized into a principle.
The Nobel Prize in Literature, awarded in 2016, formalized what had been informally true since Highway 61 Revisited: that Dylan’s lyrics constitute a body of literary work. The award was controversial, but the controversy itself proved the point — the boundaries between “song” and “literature,” like the boundaries between “folk” and “rock” that Dylan had already demolished, turned out to be institutional conventions rather than natural categories.
See also

The color line in pop — Dylan’s absorption of Black American musical traditions (blues, gospel, R&B) into a white folk rock framework raises the same questions of transmission and appropriation that the loop raises for British bands
The songwriter-performer divide — Dylan’s treatment as a poet made self-authorship the expected norm and converted the Brill Building’s division of labor into a perceived artistic deficiency
The transatlantic feedback loop — Dylan’s influence on the British Invasion, and the British bands’ subsequent influence back on American rock, is one of the loop’s central circuits
